topics.title: Introduction: Classification of Engineering Industry, Manufacturing Processes, Hot Working, Foundry Operations, Furnace and Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT834 - HAZARD CONTROL IN INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction: Classification of Engineering Industry, Manufacturing Processes, Hot Working, Foundry Operations, Furnace and Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Engineering Industry – Formal KTU Definition

An **engineering industry** is a sector of the economy that systematically converts raw materials (metals, polymers, ceramics, composites) into finished or semi-finished engineered products through the application of mechanical, thermal, chemical, and electrical processes. From the KTU 2024 Scheme perspective, an industry is classified not merely by its output but by the **nature of hazards** it generates, which directly govern the occupational health and safety (OHS) control strategy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The classification is the foundational base on which every subsequent hazard (physical, chemical, mechanical, ergonomic) is mapped. Memorize the taxonomy; every answer in ESE begins with classification context.

---

### 1.2 Classification of Engineering Industry

**A. Traditional (Classical) Classification**

| Tier | Industry Type | Definition | Example | Dominant Hazard |
|------|---------------|------------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Primary** | Extraction of raw materials from nature | Mining, quarrying, oil drilling | Dust, roof collapse, explosion |
| 2 | **Secondary** | Manufacturing / value-addition through processing | Steel plant, textile mill, foundry | Heat, fumes, machinery |
| 3 | **Tertiary** | Service & distribution | Logistics, banking, hospitals | Ergonomic, psychosocial |

**B. KTU-2024 Functional Classification (Most Frequently Asked)**

| Category | Defining Feature | Examples |
|----------|------------------|----------|
| **Heavy Industry** | Large capital, bulky raw material, high energy | Steel, shipbuilding, cement |
| **Light Industry** | Small capital, light raw material | Electronics, garments |
| **Process Industry** | Continuous/flow production; output measured in volume | Petrochemical, sugar, fertilizer |
| **Manufacturing Industry** | Discrete unit production; output measured in count | Automotive, machine tools |
| **Hazardous Industry** | Manufacture / store explosives, chemicals, gases | Ammonia, LPG, fireworks factories |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of industry like a hospital — *Primary* is the emergency room (raw extraction), *Secondary* is the operation theatre (manufacturing), and *Tertiary* is the pharmacy / billing desk (services). A safety officer must know which "ward" they are in before prescribing PPE.

**C. Classification Based on Material Handled**
- **Metallurgical Industry** – Iron, steel, non-ferrous metals
- **Chemical Industry** – Acids, alkalis, petrochemicals
- **Textile Industry** – Fiber-to-fabric chain
- **Construction Industry** – Civil works
- **Power Industry** – Generation, transmission

---

### 1.3 Manufacturing Processes – Overview

Manufacturing is the act of converting raw material into a finished product of defined geometry, tolerance, and surface finish. The KTU 2024 syllabus groups manufacturing into **four functional families**, each with a unique hazard profile.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Manufacturing Family vs Hazard Family Mapping (High-Yield for ESE):**
> 1. **Primary Shaping** → Dust, heat, molten metal
> 2. **Secondary Shaping (Machining/Forming)** → Sharp edges, flying chips, noise
> 3. **Joining** → UV radiation, fumes, electric shock
> 4. **Finishing / Surface Treatment** → Solvent vapors, chromate exposure

### 1.4 Hot Working – Definition & Intuition

**Hot Working** is the plastic deformation of a metal carried out at a temperature **above its recrystallization temperature**, where the metal can be shaped without strain hardening.

$$\boxed{T_{hot} \geq 0.6 \, T_m \;\; (T_m \text{ in Kelvin})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine clay — when cold, it cracks if you bend it; but warm it up, and it flows like putty. Metals behave the same way. "Hot working" simply means *shaping metals while they are thermally softened*. The recrystallization keeps the metal "young" so cracks don't accumulate.

**Examples:** Forging, Hot Rolling, Hot Extrusion, Hot Drawing, Piercing.

### 1.5 Foundry Operations – Definition & Intuition

A **foundry** is a manufacturing facility dedicated to producing **castings** — solid metal objects of desired shape obtained by pouring molten metal into a mold of appropriate cavity, allowing it to solidify, and then breaking out (or shake-out) the casting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** A foundry is essentially a "metal bakery." Instead of flour → dough → oven → bread, the foundry follows: **mold sand → mold cavity → molten metal → cooled casting**. The "oven" is the furnace, the "flour" is the metal charge, and the "baking tray" is the flask.

**Sequential Operations (KtU-Exam Favorite):**
1. Pattern making
2. Sand preparation & core making
3. Molding
4. Melting (in furnace)
5. Pouring
6. Solidification & cooling
7. **Shake-out** (mold removal)
8. **Fettling / Cleaning** (removing gates, risers, scale)
9. Inspection

### 1.6 Furnace and Equipment – Definition

A **furnace** is an enclosed thermal device used to generate and transfer heat to a charge (metal, ceramic, or chemical feedstock) to achieve melting, refining, or heat-treatment. In a foundry, the furnace is the *heart of the operation* and also the *largest single source of hazard*.

**Major Foundry Furnace Types:**
- Cupola Furnace
- Induction Furnace (Coreless / Channel)
- Electric Arc Furnace (EAF)
- Resistance / Crucible Furnace
- Reverberatory Furnace

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Logic – Stepwise Decision Tree

Engineering industries are classified on four principal axes. Each axis dictates the **statutory license category** under the Factories Act, 1948 (India) — directly relevant to KTU OHS modules.

**Axis 1 – Capital & Scale**
- **Micro**: Investment ≤ ₹1 Cr (manufacturing) → low-hazard cottage
- **Small**: ₹1–10 Cr → moderate hazard
- **Medium**: ₹10–50 Cr → scheduled industry
- **Large**: > ₹50 Cr → major hazard installation (MHI)

**Axis 2 – Raw Material State**
- Solid-handling → Mechanical hazard dominant
- Liquid-handling → Burn / splash hazard
- Gas-handling → Asphyxiation / explosion dominant
- Powder-handling → Dust explosion / inhalation

**Axis 3 – Process Continuity**
- Continuous process (24×7) → Cumulative exposure hazard
- Batch process → Peak-exposure hazard
- Job-shop (custom) → Variable, hard-to-standardize hazard

**Axis 4 – Worker Density per sq. m**
- High density (textile, electronics assembly) → Ergonomic, fire
- Low density (steel, cement) → Heavy-machinery, vehicular

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** Indian statutory rules (Factories Act 1948, Manufacture Storage & Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989) classify industries by these very axes. A 14-mark KTU question often asks the student to **link hazard type to industry class**.

---

### 2.2 Manufacturing Process Tree (KTU-Exam Blueprint)

A manufacturing process converts material → geometry. The standard KTU taxonomy is **Four-Family Model**:

```
1. PRIMARY SHAPING (gives geometry from raw stock)
       ├─ Casting (sand, die, investment, centrifugal)
       ├─ Powder Metallurgy (sintering)
       └─ Forming (forging, rolling, extrusion, drawing)

2. SECONDARY SHAPING (refines geometry & tolerance)
       ├─ Machining (turning, milling, drilling, grinding)
       └─ Advanced (EDM, AJM, LBM, USM)

3. JOINING (combines two or more parts)
       ├─ Welding (arc, gas, resistance, laser)
       ├─ Brazing & Soldering
       └─ Mechanical (riveting, bolting, fastening)

4. FINISHING & SURFACE TREATMENT
       ├─ Cleaning (pickling, sandblasting)
       ├─ Coating (painting, electroplating, anodizing)
       └─ Heat Treatment (annealing, hardening, tempering)
```

**Hazard Cross-Reference (High-Yield Table):**

| Process Family | Primary Hazard | Secondary Hazard | Standard PPE |
|----------------|----------------|------------------|--------------|
| Primary Shaping | Heat, dust | Noise, vibration | Apron, respirator |
| Secondary Shaping | Flying chips | Sharp edges, coolant mist | Goggles, gloves |
| Joining | UV, fume | Electric shock | Welding helmet, leathers |
| Finishing | Chemical vapor | Skin contact | Respirator, chemical suit |

---

### 2.3 Hot Working – Physics & Thermodynamics

#### 2.3.1 Why Heat is Required

Below the recrystallization temperature, every plastic deformation creates new dislocations → **strain hardening (work hardening)** → brittleness → cracking. Heat causes continuous recrystallization, restoring ductility.

**Mathematical Criterion:**

$$T_{rec} \approx 0.4 \, T_m$$

$$T_{hot\,work} \geq T_{rec}$$

#### 2.3.2 Hot Working Processes — Stepwise Logic

1. **Heating** — Charge heated above $T_{rec}$ in a furnace (fuel-fired or electric).
2. **Soaking** — Held at temperature for uniform heat penetration; otherwise, core remains cold → cracks.
3. **Deformation** — Mechanical force applied (hammer, press, rollers, die).
4. **Cooling** — Controlled to avoid residual stress; sometimes air-cooled (normalize) or quenched.

#### 2.3.3 Hot vs Cold Working (Comparison Table — KTU Favorite)

| Parameter | Hot Working | Cold Working |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| Temperature | ≥ 0.6 Tₘ | Room temperature |
| Force required | Low | High |
| Ductility | High | Limited |
| Surface finish | Poor (scale) | Excellent |
| Dimensional accuracy | Poor | Excellent |
| Strength | Lower (annealed) | Higher (strain-hardened) |
| Grain structure | Refined, equiaxed | Elongated, directional |
| Typical products | Rails, plates, structural sections | Sheets, foils, wires |

---

### 2.4 Foundry Operations – Detailed Process Logic

#### 2.4.1 Pattern Making

A **pattern** is a replica of the casting (slightly oversized to account for shrinkage). Materials: wood, metal, plastic, plaster.

#### 2.4.2 Sand Preparation

Green sand = silica sand (SiO₂) + bentonite clay (binder) + water + coal dust (carbonaceous additive).

#### 2.4.3 Molding

**Mold** = flask (upper = cope, lower = drag) + sand + parting compound. A **core** forms internal cavities.

#### 2.4.4 Melting

Conducted in furnaces (discussed in §2.5). Charge composition is critical to final casting quality.

#### 2.4.5 Pouring

Molten metal transferred from furnace to ladle to pouring basin to sprue → runner → gate → cavity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why pouring is hazardous:** Molten metal temperature for cast iron ≈ **1300–1450 °C**; for steel ≈ **1500–1650 °C**. A single splash causes third-degree burns. Moisture in the mold on contact → **steam explosion** ("blow").

#### 2.4.6 Solidification & Cooling

Casting loses superheat, then latent heat, then sensible heat. **Risers** feed molten metal to compensate shrinkage.

#### 2.4.7 Shake-out & Fettling

Casting is removed from the sand, gates and risers cut off, surface cleaned (shot blasting, grinding).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** The question *"List the operations in a foundry"* must always be answered **in process sequence**, not in random order. Random listing = 2 marks lost.

---

### 2.5 Furnace — Equipment Classification Logic

Furnaces are classified on **three orthogonal axes**:

**Axis 1 – Heat Source**
- **Fuel-fired** (coke, oil, gas)
- **Electric** (resistance, induction, arc)

**Axis 2 – Operating Mode**
- **Batch** (intermittent charging)
- **Continuous** (charge moves through)

**Axis 3 – Function**
- **Melting furnace** (cupola, induction, arc)
- **Heat-treatment furnace** (annealing, hardening)
- **Reheating furnace** (for hot working)

**Detailed Table (KTU High-Yield):**

| Furnace | Heat Source | Use | Temperature | Hazard |
|---------|-------------|-----|-------------|--------|
| **Cupola** | Coke + air blast | Cast iron melting | 1400–1500 °C | CO, dust |
| **Induction (coreless)** | Eddy currents | Steel, alloy melting | 1600 °C+ | EMF, fume |
| **Electric Arc (EAF)** | Graphite electrodes | Steelmaking | 1600–1800 °C | UV, noise, fume |
| **Resistance** | Resistance heating | Heat treatment | 800–1100 °C | Electric shock |
| **Crucible** | Coke/oil/gas | Non-ferrous melting | 700–1100 °C | Burn, fume |
| **Reverberatory** | Flame over charge | Aluminum, copper | 700–1200 °C | Flame, dust |

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` and `\mid` — never `\vert` inside table cells. The following table is rendered in LaTeX-safe form.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|------|
| 1 | Hot working threshold | $T_{hot} \geq 0.6 \, T_m$ | $T_m$ = melting point | K |
| 2 | Recrystallization | $T_{rec} \approx 0.4 \, T_m$ | — | K |
| 3 | Solidification shrinkage (volumetric) | $\Delta V / V = (\rho_s - \rho_l)/\rho_s$ | $\rho$ = density | — |
| 4 | Heat absorbed in melting | $Q = m (c \, \Delta T + L_f)$ | $L_f$ = latent heat of fusion | J |
| 5 | Furnace efficiency | $\eta = Q_{absorbed} / Q_{supplied} \times 100$ | — | % |
| 6 | Heat-transfer rate (conduction) | $\dot{Q} = kA \, \Delta T / x$ | $k$ = thermal conductivity | W |
| 7 | Heat-transfer rate (convection) | $\dot{Q} = hA (T_s - T_\infty)$ | $h$ = convection coefficient | W |
| 8 | Stack heat loss | $Q_{stack} = \dot{m}_{gas} \, c_p (T_{stack} - T_{ref})$ | — | W |
| 9 | Air-fuel ratio (stoichiometric) | $A/F = (m_{air}/m_{fuel})_{stoich}$ | — | — |
| 10 | Minimum explosive concentration | $C_{min} \leq C \leq C_{max}$ | dust/gas | g/m³ or % vol |

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Foundry hazard mapping** – Every OSHA / Indian Factory Inspector report begins with industry class.
2. **Hot-work permit system** – Hot work above ignition temperature requires written permit.
3. **Furnace selection** – Determines pollution control equipment (bag filter for cupola, scrubber for EAF).
4. **Process FMEA** – Each manufacturing family has a distinct Failure Mode taxonomy.
5. **Insurance underwriting** – Premiums depend on industry class + process + furnace type.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section develops the **analytical core** of the topic. Each derivation is fully expanded so a KTU student can reproduce every line in the answer booklet.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hot Working Temperature Criterion

**Statement:** A metal is said to be hot-worked when the working temperature exceeds **0.6 times the melting temperature (in Kelvin)**.

**Step 1 — Recall the empirical rule from physical metallurgy:**

$$\frac{T_{work}}{T_m} \geq 0.6$$

**Step 2 — Convert melting point to Kelvin:**

$$T_m (K) = T_m (°C) + 273$$

**Step 3 — Compute for mild steel (melting point ≈ 1500 °C):**

$$T_m = 1500 + 273 = 1773 \, K$$

**Step 4 — Compute minimum hot-working temperature:**

$$T_{hot} = 0.6 \times 1773 = 1063.8 \, K$$

**Step 5 — Convert back to °C for practical use:**

$$T_{hot}(°C) = 1063.8 - 273 \approx 790 \, °C$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{T_{hot\,steel} \geq 790\,°C \quad \text{(typical forging range 900–1200 °C)}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Solidification Shrinkage (Volumetric)

A molten metal contracts on freezing because the solid phase is denser than the liquid phase.

**Step 1 — Define density ratio:**

$$\rho_s > \rho_l$$

**Step 2 — Mass conservation:**

$$m = \rho_l V_l = \rho_s V_s$$

**Step 3 — Solve for solid volume:**

$$V_s = \frac{\rho_l}{\rho_s} V_l$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric shrinkage fraction:**

$$\beta = \frac{V_l - V_s}{V_l} = 1 - \frac{\rho_l}{\rho_s} = \frac{\rho_s - \rho_l}{\rho_s}$$

**Step 5 — Final form:**

$$\boxed{\beta = \frac{\rho_s - \rho_l}{\rho_s}}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example — Cast Iron:**

Take $\rho_l = 6800 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ and $\rho_s = 7200 \, \text{kg/m}^3$:

$$\beta = \frac{7200 - 6800}{7200} = \frac{400}{7200} = 0.0556 = 5.56\%$$

This shrinkage must be compensated by **risers** in the foundry.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heat Required to Melt a Metal Charge

**Statement:** Determine the total thermal energy required to raise cold scrap from room temperature to pouring temperature and fully melt it.

**Step 1 — Identify the three thermal stages:**

1. **Sensible heat to reach melting point:** $Q_1 = m \, c_{ps} (T_m - T_0)$
2. **Latent heat of fusion at $T_m$:** $Q_2 = m \, L_f$
3. **Sensible heat to reach pouring temperature (superheat):** $Q_3 = m \, c_{pl} (T_{pour} - T_m)$

**Step 2 — Sum total heat:**

$$Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3$$

**Step 3 — Factor out mass:**

$$Q_{total} = m \left[ c_{ps} (T_m - T_0) + L_f + c_{pl} (T_{pour} - T_m) \right]$$

**Step 4 — For ferrous alloys, the practical combined formula is often written as:**

$$Q_{total} = m \left[ c_{avg} (T_{pour} - T_0) + L_f \right]$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example — 1000 kg of steel:**

Given:
- $c_{avg} = 670 \, \text{J/kg·K}$
- $T_{pour} = 1600\,°C$, $T_0 = 25\,°C$
- $L_f = 2.7 \times 10^5 \, \text{J/kg}$

$$Q_1 = 1000 \times 670 \times (1600 - 25)$$

$$Q_1 = 1000 \times 670 \times 1575$$

$$Q_1 = 1.055 \times 10^9 \, \text{J}$$

$$Q_2 = 1000 \times 2.7 \times 10^5 = 2.7 \times 10^8 \, \text{J}$$

$$Q_{total} = 1.055 \times 10^9 + 2.7 \times 10^8 = 1.325 \times 10^9 \, \text{J}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J):**

$$Q_{total} = \frac{1.325 \times 10^9}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 368 \, \text{kWh}$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:** This is the *minimum theoretical* energy. Actual furnace energy consumption will be 1.5–2.5 times this value due to stack losses, radiation, and wall losses.

$$\boxed{Q_{total} = m \left[ c_{avg}(T_{pour} - T_0) + L_f \right] \quad \text{(Joules)}}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Furnace Thermal Efficiency

**Step 1 — Define supplied heat (input from fuel or electricity):**

$$Q_{supplied} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \times GCV \quad \text{(fuel-fired)}$$

or

$$Q_{supplied} = V \times I \times \cos\phi \times t \quad \text{(electric)}$$

**Step 2 — Define useful heat (absorbed by the charge):**

$$Q_{useful} = m \, c_{avg} (T_{final} - T_{initial}) + m \, L_f$$

**Step 3 — Efficiency definition:**

$$\eta = \frac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{supplied}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Typical values:**

$$\eta_{cupola} \approx 35\text{–}45\%$$

$$\eta_{induction} \approx 60\text{–}75\%$$

$$\eta_{arc} \approx 50\text{–}65\%$$

**Step 5 — Energy loss breakdown (analytical matrix):**

| Loss Type | Magnitude | Mitigation |
|-----------|-----------|------------|
| Stack loss (hot flue gas) | 25–40 % | Recuperator, preheated air |
| Wall radiation | 5–10 % | Insulating refractory |
| Charge cooling | 5 % | Continuous operation |
| Other (opening, leak) | 5–10 % | Door seals, control |

$$\boxed{\eta = 1 - \sum (\text{loss fractions})}$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Air-Fuel Ratio for Combustion Stoichiometry

**Step 1 — Combustion reaction for carbon (Coke in cupola):**

$$\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$$

**Step 2 — Molecular weights:**

$$M_C = 12, \quad M_{O_2} = 32, \quad M_{CO_2} = 44$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric oxygen per kg of carbon:**

$$\frac{32}{12} = 2.667 \, \text{kg O}_2 \text{ per kg C}$$

**Step 4 — Air contains ≈ 23 % O₂ by mass; thus:**

$$\frac{2.667}{0.23} = 11.6 \, \text{kg air per kg C}$$

**Step 5 — Final stoichiometric air-fuel ratio:**

$$\boxed{(A/F)_{stoich} \approx 11.6 \;\; \text{(kg air / kg coke)}}$$

**Step 6 — Practical cupola operation uses excess air of 10–30 % to ensure complete combustion:**

$$A/F_{actual} = (A/F)_{stoich} \times (1.1 \text{ to } 1.3)$$

---

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Industry Class vs Hazard Priority

| Industry Class | Primary Process | Dominant Hazard | Secondary Hazard | Statutory Reference |
|----------------|-----------------|-----------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Heavy Metallurgical | Hot work, casting | Heat, burns | Noise, dust | Factories Act §22 |
| Process (Chemical) | Continuous reactor | Toxic release | Fire | MSIHC Rules 1989 |
| Light Manufacturing | Machining, assembly | Mechanical | Ergonomic | Factories Act §21 |
| Foundry | Casting | Fume, explosion | Heat | Factories Act §36 |
| Heavy Engineering | Forging, rolling | Noise, vibration | Fire | Factories Act §14 |

---

### 3.7 Worked Example — Selecting Furnace for a Job

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):** A small-scale foundry wishes to melt 500 kg of non-ferrous aluminum alloy (melting point 660 °C, pouring temperature 720 °C, specific heat 900 J/kg·K, latent heat 4.0 × 10⁵ J/kg) every 4 hours. Recommend a suitable furnace type and calculate the energy required per batch.

**Step 1 — Choose furnace type:**
Non-ferrous, low melting point, intermittent batch → **Crucible furnace (coke or oil fired)** is ideal.

**Step 2 — Compute energy required:**

$$Q = m \left[ c (T_{pour} - T_0) + L_f \right]$$

$$Q = 500 \times \left[ 900 \times (720 - 25) + 4.0 \times 10^5 \right]$$

$$Q = 500 \times \left[ 900 \times 695 + 4.0 \times 10^5 \right]$$

$$Q = 500 \times \left[ 6.255 \times 10^5 + 4.0 \times 10^5 \right]$$

$$Q = 500 \times 1.0255 \times 10^6 = 5.13 \times 10^8 \, \text{J}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kWh:**

$$Q = \frac{5.13 \times 10^8}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 142.5 \, \text{kWh}$$

**Step 4 — With 50 % furnace efficiency:**

$$Q_{input} = \frac{142.5}{0.5} = 285 \, \text{kWh per batch}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** A **crucible furnace** of approximately **300 kWh capacity** is recommended, with appropriate fume extraction (aluminum dross reacts violently with water).

$$\boxed{Q_{input} \approx 285 \, \text{kWh per batch}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Furnace Equipment Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    F[Furnace Equipment] --> F1[Heat Source Type]
    F --> F2[Operating Mode]
    F --> F3[Function]

    F1 --> F1A[Fuel Fired]

    F1A --> F1Aa[Cupola]
    F1A --> F1Ab[Crucible]
    F1A --> F1Ac[Reverberatory]

    F1B --> F1Ba[Induction Coreless]
    F1B --> F1Bb[Induction Channel]
    F1B --> F1Bc[Electric Arc]
    F1B --> F1Bd[Resistance]

    F2 --> F2A[Batch]
    F2 --> F2B[Continuous]

    F3 --> F3A[Melting]
    F3 --> F3B[Heat Treatment]
    F3 --> F3C[Reheating]

    F1Aa --> F3A
    F1Ba --> F3A
    F1Bc --> F3A
    F1Bd --> F3B
```

---

### 4.5 Diagram 5 — Hot Working vs Cold Working Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Incoming Metal Stock] --> Q1{Temperature above 0.6 Tm?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> HW[Hot Working Path]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Significant Strain Required?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> CW[Cold Working Path]
    Q2 -- No --> ST[Stress Relief or Anneal]

    HW --> HW1[Heating Furnace]
    HW1 --> HW2[Soaking Zone]
    HW2 --> HW3[Deformation Process]
    HW3 --> HW4[Cooling Controlled]

    CW --> CW1[Anneal if needed]
    CW1 --> CW2[Deformation at Room Temp]
    CW2 --> CW3[Optional Intermediate Anneal]
    CW3 --> CW4[Final Hardened Part]

    HW3 -. Hazard .-> HW_HZ[Heat, Scale, Burns]
    CW2 -. Hazard .-> CW_HZ[Tool Wear, Noise, Sharp Edges]
```

---

### 4.6 Diagram 6 — Hazard Mapping for a Typical Foundry Floor

```mermaid
graph TD
    HF[Foundry Floor Hazard Map] --> HF1[Physical Hazards]
    HF --> HF2[Chemical Hazards]
    HF --> HF3[Mechanical Hazards]
    HF --> HF4[Ergonomic Hazards]

    HF1 --> HF1A[Heat Stress]
    HF1 --> HF1B[Noise]
    HF1 --> HF1C[Radiation]

    HF2 --> HF2A[Silica Dust]
    HF2 --> HF2B[Metal Fume]
    HF2 --> HF2C[CO from Cupola]

    HF3 --> HF3A[Overhead Crane]
    HF3 --> HF3B[Conveyor]
    HF3 --> HF3C[Molten Metal Splash]

    HF4 --> HF4A[Lifting Ladles]
    HF4 --> HF4B[Posture Repetition]

    HF1A --> PPE1[PPE: Heat Resistant Suit]
    HF2A --> PPE2[PPE: FFP2 Respirator]
    HF3C --> PPE3[PPE: Face Shield and Spats]
    HF1B --> PPE4[PPE: Ear Plugs]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are modeled on actual KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) paper patterns: Part A (3 marks × 2 = 6 marks), Part B (14 marks with internal choice = 14 marks). Total marks considered per answer block: **20 marks**, mirroring KTU OESFT834 ESE pattern.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Classify engineering industries into any three categories with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Engineering industries can be classified as:

1. **Heavy Industry** – Uses large quantities of raw material, energy, and capital. *Example:* Integrated Steel Plant (e.g., SAIL Bhilai).
2. **Process Industry** – Continuous-flow production measured in volume, not discrete units. *Example:* Petroleum refinery.
3. **Manufacturing Industry** – Discrete unit production measured in count. *Example:* Automotive assembly plant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Award 1 mark per category with example. No example → ½ credit.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define hot working. State the temperature criterion used to decide hot working.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Hot working is the plastic deformation of a metal carried out at a temperature sufficiently high so that the rate of recrystallization equals or exceeds the rate of strain hardening, thereby preventing work hardening during the process.

**Criterion (1.5 marks):** Hot working is performed at a temperature:

$$T_{work} \geq 0.6 \, T_m \quad (T_m \text{ in Kelvin})$$

For mild steel ($T_m \approx 1500\,°C$), hot working range begins around **790 °C**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 marks) — Recommended Attempt**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

(a) *Explain with neat sketches the classification of engineering industries. Discuss the statutory importance of classification under Indian Factory Legislation. (7 marks)*

(b) *List and explain the four families of manufacturing processes. Provide one example of each and identify the dominant hazard. (7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Introductory definition (1 mark):**
An engineering industry is a unit engaged in the conversion of raw material into finished or semi-finished goods through mechanical, thermal, chemical, or electrical processes. The classification of such industries is essential to **identify, anticipate, and control occupational hazards**.

**Step 2 — Classification axes (3 marks):**

1. **Capital-based:** Micro, Small, Medium, Large (₹1 Cr / 10 Cr / 50 Cr thresholds for manufacturing units).
2. **Process-based:** Continuous (process) vs Discrete (manufacturing) industries.
3. **Raw-material based:** Heavy (steel) vs Light (electronics) industries.
4. **Hazard-based:** Hazardous industries (explosives, gases, chemicals) under MSIHC Rules 1989.
5. **Sector-based:** Primary (extraction), Secondary (manufacturing), Tertiary (service).

**Step 3 — Statutory importance (2 marks):**
- **Factories Act, 1948** — Defines hazardous manufacturing, mandates welfare, safety, and health provisions.
- **MSIHC Rules, 1989** — Identifies "Major Hazard Installations" based on industry class and inventory.
- **Environment Protection Act, 1986** — Categorizes industries as Red, Orange, Green, White based on pollution index.
- **Statutory classification drives inspection frequency, license fees, mandatory PPE, and emergency planning.**

**Step 4 — Concluding statement (1 mark):**
Proper classification is therefore the **first step of any hazard-control program** and forms the foundation of risk assessment and mitigation in KTU Module 1.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Introductory frame (1 mark):**
Manufacturing processes convert raw material into defined geometry, tolerance, and surface finish. The KTU 2024 Scheme groups them into four families.

**Step 2 — Four families with example and hazard (4 × 1 mark = 4 marks):**

| # | Family | Process | Example | Dominant Hazard |
|---|--------|---------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Primary Shaping | Casting | Sand-cast iron pulley | Heat, dust, explosion |
| 2 | Secondary Shaping | Machining | Lathe turning | Flying chips, sharp edges |
| 3 | Joining | Welding | Arc welding of structural steel | UV radiation, fumes |
| 4 | Finishing | Painting / Plating | Electroplating of fasteners | VOC, chemical splash |

**Step 3 — Linking hazard to control (1.5 marks):**
Each family demands a tailored PPE and engineering control matrix. For example:
- Welding → Auto-darkening helmet, local exhaust ventilation.
- Machining → Machine guard, eye protection, chip disposal system.
- Casting → Heat-resistant apron, full-face shield, foot spats, respirator for silica.

**Step 4 — Concluding statement (0.5 mark):**
The four-family model is the **diagnostic backbone** of hazard identification in any manufacturing facility.

---

#### **Alternative Choice — Question B (14 marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

(a) *Define hot working. With the help of a temperature criterion, derive the hot-working temperature range for mild steel. List four hot-working processes with one industrial application each. (7 marks)*

(b) *Explain the step-by-step operations carried out in a sand foundry. Identify the major hazards at each step. (7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
Hot working is plastic deformation of metal carried out above the recrystallization temperature, where the metal continuously softens during deformation.

**Step 2 — Temperature criterion derivation (2 marks):**
The empirical rule is $T_{work} \geq 0.6 \, T_m$, with $T_m$ in Kelvin.

For mild steel, $T_m \approx 1500\,°C = 1773 \, K$.

$$T_{hot} = 0.6 \times 1773 = 1063.8 \, K = 790.8\,°C$$

So mild steel is hot-worked typically in the range **900 °C to 1200 °C** (recrystallization kinetics).

**Step 3 — Four hot-working processes (4 × 1 mark = 4 marks):**

| # | Process | Description | Industrial Application |
|---|---------|-------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Hot Rolling | Pass between rollers | Steel plates, sheets |
| 2 | Hot Forging | Compress under hammer/press | Crankshafts, connecting rods |
| 3 | Hot Extrusion | Force through die | Aluminum sections |
| 4 | Hot Drawing | Pull through die | Tubes, wires |

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Sequential operations (5 marks):**

1. **Pattern Making** – A wooden / metal replica of the casting.
2. **Sand Preparation** – Mix silica sand + bentonite + water + coal dust.
3. **Core Making** – Sand cores form internal cavities.
4. **Molding** – Pattern placed in flask, sand rammed, pattern withdrawn.
5. **Melting** – Charge melted in furnace (cupola / induction).
6. **Pouring** – Molten metal transferred to mold via ladle.
7. **Solidification & Cooling** – Metal freezes; risers feed shrinkage.
8. **Shake-out** – Casting removed from mold.
9. **Fettling** – Gates, risers, scale removed; surface cleaned.
10. **Inspection** – Dimensional and visual checks.

**Step 2 — Hazard at each step (2 marks):**

| Operation | Hazard |
|-----------|--------|
| Pattern making | Wood dust |
| Sand preparation | Silica dust |
| Molding | Vibration, dust |
| Melting | Heat, CO, fume |
| Pouring | Molten metal splash, steam blow |
| Shake-out | Dust, heat |
| Fettling | Flying chips, sparks |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Read Carefully Before Writing ESE):**
>
> 1. **Classification confusion:** Students write "Primary = manufacturing, Secondary = agriculture". This is **reversed**. Primary = extraction. (Loss: 2 marks)
> 2. **Temperature unit trap:** Writing $T_{hot} \geq 0.6 \, T_m$ with $T_m$ in °C gives wrong answer. **Always convert to Kelvin first.** (Loss: 1 mark)
> 3. **Foundry order:** Random listing of foundry operations loses sequencing credit. **Always chronologically list.**
> 4. **Hot vs Cold working comparison:** Forgetting to mention **recrystallization** as the underlying mechanism loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Furnace type identification:** Writing "induction furnace" without specifying **coreless** or **channel** is incomplete for full credit.
> 6. **PPE mapping:** Listing hazards without suggesting **control measure or PPE** is treated as descriptive-only; loses application marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read on the morning of ESE:**

- [ ] **Industry classification** → Primary, Secondary, Tertiary (sector-based) **OR** Heavy, Light, Process, Manufacturing, Hazardous (function-based).
- [ ] **Statutory link** → Factories Act 1948; MSIHC Rules 1989; EPA 1986.
- [ ] **Manufacturing four families** → Primary shaping, Secondary shaping, Joining, Finishing.
- [ ] **Hot working criterion** → $T \geq 0.6 \, T_m$ (Kelvin); mild steel starts ≈ 790 °C.
- [ ] **Recrystallization** → $T_{rec} \approx 0.4 \, T_m$.
- [ ] **Hot working processes** → Forging, Rolling, Extrusion, Drawing, Piercing.
- [ ] **Hot vs Cold** → Hot = low force, poor finish, refined grain, ductile. Cold = high force, excellent finish, strain-hardened.
- [ ] **Foundry operations** → Pattern → Sand → Core → Mold → Melt → Pour → Solidify → Shake-out → Fettling → Inspect.
- [ ] **Foundry hazards** → Silica dust (silicosis risk), molten metal splash, CO from cupola, steam blow, dust explosion.
- [ ] **Furnace types** → Cupola (cast iron), Induction (steel), EAF (steel), Reverberatory (non-ferrous), Resistance (heat treatment), Crucible (small foundry).
- [ ] **Furnace efficiency** → Cupola 35–45 %, Induction 60–75 %, EAF 50–65 %.
- [ ] **Solidification shrinkage** → $\beta = (\rho_s - \rho_l)/\rho_s$ — compensated by risers.
- [ ] **Heat for melting** → $Q = m [c(T_{pour} - T_0) + L_f]$.
- [ ] **Stoichiometric A/F (carbon)** → ≈ 11.6 kg air/kg coke.
- [ ] **Hazard-control mapping** → Every process family ↔ a standard PPE matrix.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Health Hazards
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT834 - HAZARD CONTROL IN INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Health Hazards

# 🩺 HEALTH HAZARDS – A Comprehensive KTU Engineering Study Module

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HEALTH HAZARD (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A *Health Hazard* is defined as any physical, chemical, biological, ergonomic, or psychosocial agent or condition present in the workplace that has the potential to cause occupational illness, injury, disease, impairment, or significant discomfort to a worker through acute (short-term) or chronic (long-term) exposure. Health hazards affect the biological well-being of personnel and operate on a continuum from temporary reversible effects to permanent, irreversible physiological damage.

In the KTU 2024 OBE framework, health hazards are classified under the broader **Industrial Hygiene** domain, which follows the **ANTICIPATE → RECOGNIZE → EVALUATE → CONTROL** paradigm codified in NIOSH and OSHA reference literature.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 Conceptual Analogy – "The Silent Smoke Alarm"**
> Imagine you are cooking in a kitchen. While a **fire** is a *Safety Hazard* (sudden, catastrophic, visible), the **invisible carbon monoxide** quietly released from a gas stove is a *Health Hazard* — it doesn't explode or burn you, but slow exposure will make you drowsy, nauseous, and eventually harm you. Health hazards are the **silent, cumulative threats** of industry. They don't announce themselves; they accumulate like small debts that eventually break the body's health ledger.

### 1.3 The Five (5) Universal Categories of Health Hazards

Under the KTU 2024 OESFT834 syllabus and aligned with the internationally recognized **NIOSH Hierarchy of Health Hazards**, every workplace health hazard falls into one of the following five classifications:

| S.No. | Hazard Category | Core Definition | Common Industrial Examples |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1** | **Chemical Hazards** | Toxic substances in solid, liquid, or gaseous form | Solvents, acids, pesticides, fumes, dusts |
| **2** | **Physical Hazards** | Environmental factors causing bodily harm | Noise, vibration, radiation, temperature extremes |
| **3** | **Biological Hazards** | Living organisms or bio-active agents | Bacteria, viruses, fungi, blood-borne pathogens |
| **4** | **Ergonomic Hazards** | Workplace design causing musculoskeletal strain | Repetitive motion, poor posture, heavy lifting |
| **5** | **Psychosocial Hazards** | Work-related mental/emotional stressors | Stress, harassment, shift work, burnout |

### 1.4 Key Reference Metrics (Must Memorize for KTU Exam)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KEY INDUSTRIAL HYGIENE METRICS:**
> - **TLV (Threshold Limit Value)** = Airborne concentration (mg/m³ or ppm) of a substance to which a worker can be exposed **8 hours/day, 40 hours/week** for a working lifetime **without adverse health effects**.
> - **PEL (Permissible Exposure Limit)** = The legally enforceable OSHA limit.
> - **TWA (Time-Weighted Average)** = The average exposure over an 8-hour workday.
> - **STEL (Short-Term Exposure Limit)** = **15-minute** time-weighted average that should not be exceeded.
> - **IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health)** = Concentration posing immediate threat.
> - **LD₅₀ (Lethal Dose 50%)** = Dose causing death in 50% of test subjects (mg/kg body weight).
> - **LC₅₀ (Lethal Concentration 50%)** = Concentration in air causing death in 50% of subjects (ppm or mg/m³).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemical Hazards – The Dominant Industrial Concern

Chemical hazards constitute the **largest single category** of health hazards in KTU's industrial health syllabus. They are sub-classified by **physical state of dispersion**.

### 2.1.1 Sub-Classification of Chemical Hazards

**A) Dusts**
- Solid particles created by **mechanical processes** (grinding, drilling, crushing, sanding).
- Particle size typically **> 1 μm (micrometer)**.
- *Examples*: Coal dust (pneumoconiosis), silica dust (silicosis), asbestos (asbestosis), cotton dust (byssinosis), grain dust, wood dust.

**B) Fumes**
- Solid particles formed by **condensation of vaporized material** (welding, smelting, soldering).
- Particle size **< 1 μm** (respirable fraction).
- *Examples*: Lead fume, zinc oxide fume (metal fume fever), welding fume.

**C) Smoke**
- Carbon or soot particles from **incomplete combustion** of organic materials.
- *Examples*: Cigarette smoke, diesel exhaust, fire smoke.

**D) Gases**
- Substances in **gaseous state at room temperature**, occupying container volume.
- *Examples*: Carbon monoxide (CO), hydrogen sulfide (H₂S), chlorine (Cl₂), ammonia (NH₃).

**E) Vapors**
- **Gaseous phase of a substance that is liquid at room temperature** (e.g., solvent evaporation).
- *Examples*: Benzene vapor, toluene vapor, mercury vapor.

**F) Mists**
- Liquid droplets **suspended in air** from spraying, atomizing, or condensation.
- *Examples*: Acid mist (electroplating), paint spray mist, oil mist.

**G) Fibers**
- Elongated solid particles with **length-to-diameter ratio > 3:1**.
- *Examples*: Asbestos fibers, fiberglass, mineral wool.

### 2.1.2 The Four Routes of Entry (CRITICAL for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **THE 4 ROUTES OF CHEMICAL ENTRY INTO THE HUMAN BODY:**
> 1. **Inhalation** → Lungs → Bloodstream (MOST COMMON — accounts for ~95% of occupational poisonings).
> 2. **Skin (Dermal) Absorption** → Direct contact with skin (2nd most common).
> 3. **Ingestion (Oral)** → Hand-to-mouth transfer of contaminated materials.
> 4. **Injection** → High-pressure equipment punctures or sharp objects (rare but dangerous).

### 2.2 Physical Hazards – Engineering-Focused Category

For KTU engineering students, physical hazards require quantitative treatment.

**A) Noise**
- **Unit of measurement**: Decibels (dB).
- **Reference standard**: 90 dB(A) for 8 hours (OSHA PEL).
- **Doubling Rule**: Every **+5 dB increase** halves the safe exposure time.

**B) Vibration**
- Whole-Body Vibration (WBV) vs. Hand-Arm Vibration (HAV).
- *Unit*: m/s² RMS (Root Mean Square acceleration).
- *Common disorder*: Hand-Arm Vibration Syndrome (HAVS) / Vibration White Finger.

**C) Radiation**
- **Ionizing**: Alpha (α), Beta (β), Gamma (γ), X-rays, neutrons.
- **Non-Ionizing**: UV, visible light, infrared, microwave, radiofrequency, laser.

**D) Temperature Extremes**
- Heat stress: WBGT (Wet Bulb Globe Temperature) index used to evaluate hot environments.
- Cold stress: Frostbite, hypothermia.

**E) Pressure (Barotrauma)**
- Decompression sickness (caisson disease) in divers; high-pressure jet injuries.

### 2.3 Biological Hazards

- **Bacteria**: Legionella, anthrax, tuberculosis.
- **Viruses**: Hepatitis B/C, HIV, COVID-19.
- **Fungi**: Mold, spores.
- **Parasites**: Malaria, hookworm.
- **Insects/Animals**: Snake/scorpion bites, rodent droppings.
- **Industries affected**: Healthcare, agriculture, food processing, waste handling, animal husbandry.

### 2.4 Ergonomic Hazards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ERGONOMIC HAZARD FOCUS (KTU 2024):**
> - **Repetitive Strain Injury (RSI)**
> - **Carpal Tunnel Syndrome (CTS)**
> - **Lower Back Pain (LBP)**
> - **Vision Strain (Computer Vision Syndrome)**
> - **Slip, Trip & Fall hazards** (often classified here or under safety)

### 2.5 Psychosocial Hazards

- Workplace stress, violence, harassment, bullying.
- Shift work disorder, burnout, anxiety, depression.
- *Linked directly to*: Long working hours, job insecurity, monotonous tasks.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Application | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $C_{ppm} = \dfrac{V_{solute}}{V_{solution}} \times 10^{6}$ | Gases concentration | ppm |
| 2 | $C_{mg/m^{3}} = \dfrac{P \times MW}{R \times T}$ | Vapor concentration from partial pressure | mg/m³ |
| 3 | $\text{TWA} = \dfrac{\sum (C_i \times t_i)}{\sum t_i}$ | 8-hr Time-Weighted Average | ppm or mg/m³ |
| 4 | $\text{Habitual Exposure} = \dfrac{T_{\text{actual}}}{T_{\text{allowed}}} \times 100\%$ | Dose equivalent for non-standard shifts | % |
| 5 | $\text{Dose (Hygienists)} = C \times T$ | Exposure dose in toxicology | ppm·hr or mg/m³·hr |
| 6 | $\text{Noise: } T_{\text{allowed}} = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}}$ | OSHA safe duration at level $L$ dB(A) | hours |
| 7 | $\text{Heat Stress Index: } \text{WBGT} = 0.7\,T_{nwb} + 0.2\,T_{g} + 0.1\,T_{db}$ | Outdoor heat load | °C |
| 8 | $\text{Probit Equation: } P = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{Y} e^{-u^{2}/2}\,du$ | Dose-response mortality | — |
| 9 | $\text{Dose Response: } Y = a + b \ln(C^{n} t)$ | Toxicity scaling | — |
| 10 | $\text{LCt}_{50} = c \times t$ (constant product) | Haber's Law (toxic load) | ppm·min |

> [!NOTE]
> **HABER'S LAW** is foundational: $C \times t = k$ (constant). The biological effect of a toxic gas is proportional to the **product of concentration and exposure time**. This is the core principle behind TLV, STEL, and IDLH.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Time-Weighted Average (TWA) Exposure Calculation

The TWA is the single most important calculation in industrial hygiene. KTU exam problems frequently ask students to compute TWA for a worker with varying exposure concentrations.

### 3.1.1 Starting Principle

A worker is exposed to varying concentrations of a contaminant throughout their 8-hour shift. The **TWA reduces this varying exposure to one equivalent constant concentration** that represents the same biological dose.

### 3.1.2 General Mathematical Statement

$$
\text{TWA} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot t_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}
$$

Where:
- $C_i$ = Concentration during the $i^{th}$ interval (in ppm or mg/m³)
- $t_i$ = Duration of the $i^{th}$ interval (in hours)
- $n$ = Number of distinct exposure intervals
- $\sum t_i$ = Total work-shift time (typically 8 hours)

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-STYLE PROBLEM:** A worker in a chemical plant has the following exposure profile during an 8-hour shift:
> - **0–2 hr**: Exposure to 50 ppm of toluene
> - **2–4 hr**: Exposure to 90 ppm of toluene
> - **4–6 hr**: No exposure (break + non-exposed task)
> - **6–8 hr**: Exposure to 70 ppm of toluene
> 
> The OSHA PEL (TLV) for toluene is **100 ppm (8-hr TWA)**. Determine whether the worker is overexposed.

**Step 1 — Identify each exposure interval and its concentration.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= 50 \text{ ppm}, \quad t_1 = 2 \text{ hr} \\
C_2 &= 90 \text{ ppm}, \quad t_2 = 2 \text{ hr} \\
C_3 &= 0 \text{ ppm}, \quad t_3 = 2 \text{ hr} \\
C_4 &= 70 \text{ ppm}, \quad t_4 = 2 \text{ hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute each $C_i \cdot t_i$ product.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 \cdot t_1 &= 50 \times 2 = 100 \text{ ppm·hr} \\
C_2 \cdot t_2 &= 90 \times 2 = 180 \text{ ppm·hr} \\
C_3 \cdot t_3 &= 0 \times 2 = 0 \text{ ppm·hr} \\
C_4 \cdot t_4 &= 70 \times 2 = 140 \text{ ppm·hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the products (numerator).**

$$
\sum C_i \cdot t_i = 100 + 180 + 0 + 140 = 420 \text{ ppm·hr}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the time intervals (denominator).**

$$
\sum t_i = 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 8 \text{ hr}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute TWA.**

$$
\text{TWA} = \frac{420 \text{ ppm·hr}}{8 \text{ hr}} = 52.5 \text{ ppm}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare to TLV/PEL.**

$$
\text{TWA} = 52.5 \text{ ppm} \;\; < \;\; 100 \text{ ppm (PEL)} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{EXPOSURE IS WITHIN SAFE LIMITS.}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute percentage of permissible exposure.**

$$
\% \text{PEL} = \frac{52.5}{100} \times 100\% = 52.5\%
$$

> **Conclusion:** The worker is exposed to **52.5% of the permissible limit** — within the safe boundary. ✅

### 3.2 Derivation: OSHA Noise Dose Formula

OSHA's permissible noise exposure is governed by a **5 dB exchange rate** and an **8-hour reference duration**.

**Starting formula:**

$$
T_{\text{allowed}} = \frac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}}
$$

**Derivation steps:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reference level: } L_0 &= 90 \text{ dB(A) for } T_0 = 8 \text{ hr} \\
\text{Exchange rate: } q &= 5 \text{ dB per halving} \\
\text{Number of halvings from } L_0 \text{ to } L: n &= \frac{L - L_0}{q} = \frac{L - 90}{5} \\
\text{Allowed time: } T_{\text{allowed}} &= \frac{T_0}{2^{n}} = \frac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: KTU Health Hazard Comparison

| Feature | Chemical Hazard | Physical Hazard | Biological Hazard | Ergonomic Hazard |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Trigger Agent** | Toxic substance | Energy/force | Living organism | Posture/repetition |
| **Detection** | Air sampling, lab tests | Instrument reading | Microbial culture | Workstation analysis |
| **Onset** | Acute/Chronic | Usually Chronic | Variable | Chronic |
| **Affected System** | Multiple organs | Specific organ | Immune/whole body | Musculoskeletal |
| **Example Disease** | Silicosis | Noise-induced hearing loss | Legionnaires' | Carpal Tunnel Syndrome |
| **Unit Metric** | ppm, mg/m³ | dB, m/s², Sv | CFU/m³ | NIOSH Lifting Index |
| **Primary Control** | Ventilation, PPE | Engineering isolation | Vaccination, hygiene | Redesign of work |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Industrial Hygiene Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Industrial Process] --> B[Anticipate Potential Hazards]
    B --> C{Type of Hazard?}
    C -->|Chemical| D1[Air Sampling and Chemical Analysis]
    C -->|Physical| D2[Noise, Vibration, Radiation Measurement]
    C -->|Biological| D3[Microbial and Pathogen Testing]
    C -->|Ergonomic| D4[Workstation and Posture Analysis]
    C -->|Psychosocial| D5[Worker Surveys and Mental Health Assessment]
    D1 --> E[Compare Measured Value to TLV or PEL]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F{Exceeds Limit?}
    F -->|No| G[Document and Monitor Periodically]
    F -->|Yes| H[Apply Hierarchy of Controls]
    H --> H1[Elimination]
    H1 --> H2[Substitution]
    H2 --> H3[Engineering Controls]
    H3 --> H4[Administrative Controls]
    H4 --> H5[PPE as Last Resort]
    H5 --> I[Re-Evaluate Exposure Levels]
    I --> E
    G --> J[Periodic Re-Assessment Cycle]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Routes of Chemical Entry into the Human Body

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Workplace Chemical Hazard Source] --> B[Release into Environment]
    B --> C1[Inhalation Route]
    B --> C2[Dermal Skin Route]
    B --> C3[Ingestion Oral Route]
    B --> C4[Injection Route]
    C1 --> D1[Lungs Absorb into Bloodstream]
    C2 --> D2[Absorbed through Skin Layers]
    C3 --> D3[GI Tract Absorption]
    C4 --> D4[Direct Bloodstream Entry]
    D1 --> E[Systemic Toxicity and Organ Damage]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Acute Effects]
    E --> G[Chronic Effects]
    F --> H1[Headache, Dizziness, Narcosis]
    G --> H2[Cancer, Pneumoconiosis, Organ Failure]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Health Hazard Controls (KTU High-Yield)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hazard Identified Exceeding TLV or PEL] --> B[Level 1 Elimination]
    B --> C[Level 2 Substitution]
    C --> D[Level 3 Engineering Controls]
    D --> D1[Local Exhaust Ventilation]
    D --> D2[Enclosure and Isolation]
    D --> D3[Process Modification]
    D1 --> E[Level 4 Administrative Controls]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1[Job Rotation and Reduced Shift Hours]
    E --> E2[Training and Health Surveillance]
    E1 --> F[Level 5 Personal Protective Equipment PPE]
    E2 --> F
    F --> F1[Respirators and Gloves]
    F --> F2[Protective Clothing and Eyewear]
    F1 --> G[Re-Measure and Verify Compliance]
    F2 --> G
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Acute vs Chronic Health Hazard Effects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Worker Exposure Event] --> B{Duration of Exposure}
    B -->|Short Term High Dose| C[Acute Effects]
    B -->|Long Term Low Dose| D[Chronic Effects]
    C --> C1[Headache and Nausea]
    C --> C2[Chemical Burns]
    C --> C3[Acute Poisoning and Unconsciousness]
    C --> C4[Respiratory Arrest]
    D --> D1[Pneumoconiosis Silicosis Asbestosis]
    D --> D2[Occupational Cancer Mesothelioma Leukemia]
    D --> D3[Reproductive Disorders]
    D --> D4[Neurological Damage Peripheral Neuropathy]
    D --> D5[Liver and Kidney Damage]
```
