# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
**Explain the "work rest" requirement in a pedestal or bench grinder. Why is it a critical safety feature?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** A *work rest* is a rigid metallic support placed in front of a grinding wheel, between the wheel and the operator, on which the workpiece is rested during grinding.
2. **Function (1 mark):** It provides mechanical support to the workpiece, prevents it from being jammed between the wheel and the operator's hand, and limits the gap through which the hand can approach the wheel.
3. **Safety requirement (1 mark):** As per **IS 1990** and **OSHA 1910.215(a)(4)**, the gap between the work rest and the wheel periphery must not exceed **3 mm (1/8 inch)**. If the gap exceeds 3 mm, the workpiece may be pulled in by the wheel, causing *wheel jam* and disintegration.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Define "Total Productive Maintenance" (TPM). List any four pillars of TPM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Definition (2 marks):** TPM is a holistic maintenance philosophy that aims to achieve *zero breakdowns, zero defects, and zero accidents* by involving *all employees* — from top management to shop-floor operators — in the maintenance of equipment. It was developed by **Nippon Denso** (a Toyota group company) in **1971** and formalized by **JIPM**.
2. **Four pillars (1 mark):**
   - Autonomous Maintenance
   - Planned Maintenance
   - Quality Maintenance
   - Focused Improvement (Kobetsu Kaizen)
   
   *(Other acceptable pillars: Early Equipment Management, Training, SHE, Administrative TPM.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Process Planning & Grinding Hazards Focus*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question for OESFT834 Module 2]**
> 
> **(a)** Explain the **process planning steps** for a precision surface grinding operation on a hardened EN8 steel shaft. In your answer, justify the selection of the grinding wheel, dressing, and coolant strategy. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Identify the **major hazards** in surface grinding operations and describe the engineering and administrative controls that must be in place to mitigate them, with reference to relevant Indian Standards. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Process Planning for Surface Grinding of EN8 Shaft (7 marks):**

1. **Workpiece analysis (1 mark):** EN8 is a medium-carbon steel (0.4% C), hardened to 55–60 HRC, with high wear resistance. It is *not* a hazardous material (no beryllium, lead, or silica). It is, however, *hard*, requiring a *softer-grade* vitrified wheel to prevent glazing and heat damage.

2. **Wheel selection (1.5 marks):**
   - **Abrasive:** Aluminium oxide (Al₂O₃) — preferred for ferrous, tough materials.
   - **Grit size:** 46–60 (medium) for roughing; 80–120 for finishing.
   - **Grade:** K–M (medium-soft) for hardened steel; rule = *hard workpiece → soft wheel*.
   - **Structure:** 5–8 (open) for chip clearance and reduced heat.
   - **Bond:** Vitrified (withstands high temperatures, rigid, brittle-safe).

3. **Process parameters (1.5 marks):**
   - Wheel speed $v_s$ = 30–33 m/s (vitrified safe limit).
   - Workpiece speed $v_w$ = 10–20 m/min.
   - Depth of cut $a_p$ = 0.005–0.02 mm/pass.
   - Cross-feed $f_r$ = 0.5–2 mm/pass.

4. **Dressing plan (1 mark):**
   - Single-point diamond dresser, traverse rate 0.5–1 mm/rev.
   - Dress at start of shift and after every 20–30 workpieces to restore wheel sharpness.

5. **Coolant plan (1 mark):**
   - Flood coolant (synthetic or emulsion), flow **15–20 L/min**, directed at the grinding zone.
   - Functions: heat removal, chip flushing, dust suppression, surface finish improvement.

6. **Safety-in-planning (1 mark):**
   - Ring test before mounting; balance check; guard clearance ≤ 6 mm; LEV hood; PPE schedule.

**(b) Hazards and Controls in Surface Grinding (7 marks):**

1. **Wheel disintegration (1.5 marks):**
   - **Cause:** Excess $v_s$, imbalance, wrong wheel for material, damage in handling.
   - **Control:** Ring test; operate ≤ 33 m/s for vitrified; safety guard (BS EN 13218, IS 15282); wheel bursting test at 2× operating speed.
   
2. **Flying workpiece / fragments (1 mark):**
   - **Control:** Work rest gap ≤ 3 mm; clamped workpiece; tongue guard; eye protection (EN 166 B).

3. **Dust and coolant mist (1.5 marks):**
   - **Hazard:** Respiratory irritation, dermatitis, *Legionella* in stagnant coolant.
   - **Control:** LEV with **0.5 m/s capture velocity**; mist collector; biocide dosing every 2 weeks; sump cleaning monthly; respirator (EN 149 FFP2) for dust.

4. **Noise (1 mark):**
   - **Source:** Wheel-work contact, motor, coolant pump; typically 80–95 dB(A).
   - **Control:** Enclosure; hearing PPE (EN 352); reduce wheel wear via dressing; isolation; limit exposure per 3-dB rule.

5. **Fire from sparks (1 mark):**
   - **Control:** Spark arrestor; no flammable material within 1.5 m; Class B fire extinguisher (CO₂ or dry powder); housekeeping.

6. **Hand-arm vibration (1 mark):**
   - **Control:** Anti-vibration handles, low-vibration tool selection, job rotation, health surveillance when A(8) > 5 m/s².

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question A:**
> - *Common mistake 1:* Students describe the *process parameters* but forget the **safety-in-planning** step. KTU awards marks specifically for integrating OSH into the plan. (Deduct 1–2 marks.)
> - *Common mistake 2:* Citing only "PPE" as a control. KTU expects the *full Hierarchy of Controls* — give engineering and administrative controls priority. (Deduct 1 mark.)
> - *Common mistake 3:* Not quoting the **Indian Standard** (e.g., IS 1990 for work rest, IS 15282 for wheel safety). Always cite at least one standard. (Deduct 0.5–1 mark.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Maintenance & Health Hazards Focus*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question for OESFT834 Module 2]**
> 
> **(a)** Discuss the **different types of maintenance** strategies for machine tools with their relative merits and demerits. Justify which strategy is most suitable for a CNC grinding cell producing medical implants. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Explain the **physical and chemical health hazards** faced by a machine operator in a grinding shop. Describe the **occupational health surveillance** program that should be in place, with reference to the relevant Indian regulations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Maintenance Strategies (7 marks):**

| Strategy | Description | Merits | Demerits |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Corrective (Breakdown)** | Repair after failure | No upfront cost; simple | Unplanned downtime; safety risk; collateral damage |
| **Preventive (Scheduled)** | Service at fixed intervals (calendar/hours) | Reduces surprise failures; planned downtime | May replace parts early (over-maintenance cost) |
| **Predictive (Condition-based)** | Service when condition sensor triggers | "Just-in-time" parts replacement; high availability | High sensor cost; needs trained analysts |
| **Autonomous (Operator-driven)** | Operator cleans, inspects, lubricates daily | Builds ownership; detects early issues | Depends on training & discipline |
| **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** | Holistic system, 8 pillars, all employees | World-class OEE (≥ 85%); zero accidents | Slow to implement; cultural change needed |

1. **Definition block for each (2 marks):** Brief explanation of all five strategies.
2. **Comparative table (2 marks):** A clean merit/demerit summary.
3. **CNC grinding cell justification (3 marks):**
   - *Why TPM + PdM is ideal:* Medical implants demand **zero-defect**, **traceable** production. A bearing failure mid-grind on a titanium knee implant = scrap + patient risk.
   - **Vibration analysis** (ISO 10816) and **thermography** detect spindle degradation early.
   - **Autonomous maintenance** by operators for daily cleaning of swarf from slides.
   - **Planned PM** for ball-screw lubrication, encoder calibration, wheel dresser check.
   - **Compliance:** ISO 13485 (medical devices) and **ISO 9001:2015** both require documented maintenance.

**(b) Health Hazards & Surveillance (7 marks):**

1. **Physical hazards (2 marks):**
   - **Noise** from wheel-work contact (80–95 dB(A)) → NIHL.
   - **Vibration** from rotary tools → HAVS.
   - **Heat** from grinding sparks and coolant → burns.
   - **Flying fragments** → eye/face injury.
   - **Slip/trip** from coolant spillage.

2. **Chemical hazards (2 marks):**
   - **Metal dust** (iron, chromium, nickel) → pneumoconiosis, asthma, lung cancer (Cr/Ni are Group 1 IARC carcinogens).
   - **Coolant mist** (mineral oil, biocides) → dermatitis, asthma, *Legionella* in sump.
   - **Silica dust** (from sandstone wheels or cast iron grinding) → silicosis.

3. **Health surveillance program (2 marks):**
   - **Pre-employment baseline:** Audiometry, spirometry, skin examination, vision test.
   - **Periodic (annual):** Repeat audiometry (NIHL), lung function test (pneumoconiosis), finger blanching test (HAVS).
   - **Biological monitoring:** Blood/urine for chromium/nickel (if applicable).
   - **Record keeping:** As per **Factories Act 1948 Section 91** and **OSH Code 2020**, health records must be maintained for **30 years** post-employment.

4. **Regulatory framework (1 mark):**
   - **Factories Act, 1948** (Sections 11, 12, 89, 91).
   - **OSH Code, 2020** (Welfare, Health, Safety provisions).
   - **IS 8520** (Guide for noise control in industry).
   - **CPCB / Kerala PCB** for emission and effluent standards.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question B:**
> - *Common mistake 1:* Listing hazards *without* linking them to **specific Indian standards or sections of the Factories Act**. KTU marks these linkages explicitly. (Deduct 1–2 marks.)
> - *Common mistake 2:* Confusing **corrective** with **preventive** maintenance. Memory aid: *corrective = reacts; preventive = anticipates*. (Deduct 0.5 mark.)
> - *Common mistake 3:* Omitting the **TPM pillar 7 (SHE)** in the maintenance answer. KTU specifically tests this integration. (Deduct 1 mark.)
> - *Common mistake 4:* Failing to mention **Legionella** risk in coolant management — a frequent KTU "trick question" since it links microbiology with manufacturing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Use this checklist the night before the exam.**

- [ ] **Process planning is a hazard control tool**, not just a production activity — integrate OSH at *every* step.
- [ ] **Wheel peripheral speed** = $\pi D N / 60$; safe limit **33 m/s** (vitrified), bursting test at **2× operating speed**.
- [ ] **Work rest gap ≤ 3 mm**; **tongue guard gap ≤ 6 mm** (ANSI B7.1 / IS 1990).
- [ ] **Hard wheel for soft workpiece, soft wheel for hard workpiece** — the cardinal KTU rule.
- [ ] **Ring test** = mandatory pre-mounting check; produces a clear metallic ring if wheel is sound.
- [ ] **Five maintenance types:** Corrective, Preventive, Predictive, Autonomous, TPM.
- [ ] **TPM = 8 pillars**; Pillar 7 = **SHE** (Safety, Health, Environment) — equally important as technical pillars.
- [ ] **OEE = A × P × Q**; world-class ≥ **85%**.
- [ ] **Vibration severity (ISO 10816-3):** 1.4 / 2.8 / 4.5 mm/s = A/B/C/D zones.
- [ ] **Noise:** Permissible Exposure Limit = **85 dB(A) TWA / 8 h** (Factories Act / ACGIH).
- [ ] **3-dB rule:** each +3 dB halves permissible exposure time; **5-dB rule** (OSHA) is also valid to know.
- [ ] **Hand-arm vibration action value:** A(8) = **5 m/s²**; ELV = **20 m/s²** (ISO 5349).
- [ ] **Hierarchy of Controls (HoC):** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- [ ] **Source–Path–Receiver** is the industrial hygiene model; *source control* is most effective.
- [ ] **Dust standards:** Silica TLV = **0.025 mg/m³**; LEV capture velocity = **0.5 m/s** for grinding.
- [ ] **Coolant management:** Biocide every 2 weeks; sump cleaning monthly; *Legionella* risk.
- [ ] **Health surveillance records:** Retained for **30 years** post-employment (Factories Act §91).
- [ ] **Standards to quote in answers:** IS 1990, IS 15282, IS 8520, ISO 45001, ISO 10816, ISO 5349, ISO 1940-1, ANSI B7.1.
- [ ] **Key formulas (recommit to memory):**
  - $v_s = \pi D_s N_s / 60$
  - $Z_w = v_w \cdot a_p \cdot f_r$
  - $A(8) = a_{hv} \sqrt{T/8}$
  - $T = T_0 \cdot 10^{-(L - L_0)/10}$
  - $OEE = A \times P \times Q$
  - $\sigma_{\max} = \rho v_s^2$ (wheel burst stress)

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *A grinding shop is a forest of rotating energy. Every plan, every guard, every test, every record exists to keep that energy in its proper cage.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welding and Cutting: Safety Precautions of Gas Welding and Arc Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT834 - HAZARD CONTROL IN INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Welding and Cutting: Safety Precautions of Gas Welding and Arc Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Welding & Cutting Hazards

**Welding** is a fabrication process that joins materials (typically metals or thermoplastics) by causing fusion, typically achieved by heating the workpieces to their melting temperature with or without the application of pressure. **Cutting** is the controlled separation of metal using thermal energy.

In industrial hazard control, welding and cutting operations rank among the **top three causes of industrial fires and burn injuries** globally. The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes hazard control in these operations because they combine **chemical, electrical, thermal, and radiological hazards** simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 focuses on the *safety engineering* perspective of welding, NOT the metallurgical aspects. Students must emphasize hazard identification, PPE selection, fire prevention, and statutory compliance (Factories Act 1948, Indian Electricity Rules, BIS standards).

---

## 1.2 Gas Welding — Formal Definition

**Gas Welding** (also called **Oxy-Fuel Gas Welding, OFW**) is a fusion welding process in which the heat required for joining metals is obtained by the combustion of a suitable fuel gas with oxygen. The most common combination is **Oxygen + Acetylene (C₂H₂)**, commonly called the **Oxy-Acetylene process**.

**Chemical Reaction of Oxy-Acetylene Combustion:**

$$2C_2H_2 + 5O_2 \rightarrow 4CO_2 + 2H_2O + \text{Heat} \ (\approx 3200^\circ C)$$

The neutral flame temperature reaches approximately **3160 °C to 3200 °C**, making it the hottest practical gas flame.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Controlled Bonfire

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of gas welding as a *highly controlled bonfire*. A bonfire is dangerous because wood (fuel) burns with air (oxygen) and releases uncontrolled heat. In gas welding, we precisely meter pure oxygen and acetylene through narrow nozzles, controlling the fire's size, temperature, and direction. **The safety challenge is that we are deliberately creating a fire in the workplace** — so every safeguard exists to prevent this fire from escaping its intended boundary.

---

## 1.3 Arc Welding — Formal Definition

**Arc Welding** is a fusion welding process in which the heat for joining metals is produced by an **electric arc** struck between an electrode and the workpiece (or between two electrodes). The arc temperature reaches **6000 °C to 8000 °C**, far exceeding gas welding.

Common variants include:
- **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** — Manual "stick" welding
- **Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW/MIG)** — Metal Inert Gas
- **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW/TIG)** — Tungsten Inert Gas
- **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** — Flux-covered

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU Board Expectation):** *Arc welding is the process of joining metals by heating them to a molten state using an electric arc, with or without the application of pressure and with or without the use of filler metal.*

### Intuitive Analogy — The Artificial Lightning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a tiny, controlled bolt of artificial lightning. Natural lightning is terrifying because of its heat, light, and electrical energy. Arc welding creates a *miniature lightning bolt* between the electrode and metal. **The safety challenge is now compounded** — we must control not only fire and heat but also **electrocution, ultraviolet radiation (UV/IR burns to eyes — "arc eye"), and toxic fumes from molten metal**. The hazards of arc welding are multi-dimensional compared to gas welding.

---

## 1.4 Comparative Hazard Profile

| Parameter | Gas Welding (Oxy-Acetylene) | Arc Welding (SMAW) |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Energy Source** | Chemical combustion | Electrical arc |
| **Maximum Temperature** | **3200 °C** | **8000 °C** |
| **Dominant Hazard** | Fire / Explosion | Electric shock / UV radiation |
| **Fume Type** | Minimal (mostly CO₂, H₂O) | Heavy (metal oxide fumes) |
| **Noise Level** | Low (~80 dB) | High (~100–115 dB) |
| **PPE Criticality** | Eye protection (shade 4–6) | Eye protection (shade 10–13) |

---

## 1.5 Common Hazards in Welding Operations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition — "Welding Hazard Triad":**
> 1. **Physical Hazards** — Fire, explosion, electric shock, burns, radiation
> 2. **Chemical Hazards** — Fumes (Mn, Cr, Ni, ozone, NOx), gases (CO, phosgene)
> 3. **Ergonomic Hazards** — Posture, repetitive strain, heat stress

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Gas Welding Safety — Hazard Analysis

### 2.1.1 Cylinder Storage and Handling

Gas cylinders store gases at high pressure (**oxygen at 150 bar / 2175 psi**, **acetylene at 1.5 bar / 22 psi dissolved in acetone**). Mishandling causes catastrophic explosions.

**Key Safety Rules:**

- **Segregation Principle:** Oxygen cylinders must be stored **at least 6 meters (20 feet)** away from fuel gas cylinders, or separated by a **non-combustible firewall of at least 1.5 meters (5 feet) height** with a **30-minute fire rating**.
- **Chain Mounting:** Cylinders must always be secured upright with **chains or straps** to prevent toppling. A falling acetylene cylinder can rupture its valve and explode.
- **Cap Protection:** Valve protection caps must remain on cylinders when not in use.
- **Temperature Control:** Storage area temperature must NOT exceed **50 °C**. Acetylene becomes unstable above **200 °C** and can decompose explosively even without air.

### 2.1.2 Regulators and Flashback Arrestors

The **pressure regulator** reduces cylinder pressure to working pressure. **Two-stage regulators** are mandatory for oxy-acetylene work.

> [!NOTE]
> **Flashback vs. Backfire — KTU Frequently Tested Distinction:**
> - **Backfire:** The flame retreats into the nozzle tip and pops out with a loud snap. The torch remains lit. *Low severity.*
> - **Flashback:** The flame travels *upstream* into the hose or regulator. *High severity — can cause explosion in cylinder.* Flashback arrestors are mandatory safety devices.

**Flashback Arrester Placement:** Two required per torch setup:
1. At the **regulator outlet** (toroidal type)
2. At the **torch inlet** (resettable type)

### 2.1.3 Hose and Connection Safety

- Hoses must be **distinctly colored**: **Red for fuel gas (acetylene)**, **Blue (or Black) for oxygen**.
- Use only **BS EN 559 / IS 447** rated hoses.
- Maximum hose length: **recommended 10 meters** to prevent pressure drop.
- **Hose check valves** prevent backflow of gas into the wrong line.
- **Never use oil or grease** on oxygen fittings — causes spontaneous combustion (auto-ignition at **~250 °C** in pure oxygen).

### 2.1.4 Neutral Flame Chemistry

The correct oxygen-to-acetylene ratio produces a **neutral flame**:

$$\text{O}_2 : \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 = 1 : 1 \quad (\text{Neutral Flame})$$

$$Oxygen = \frac{1}{1+1} \times 100\% = 50\%\ \text{by volume}$$

| Flame Type | O₂:C₂H₂ Ratio | Temperature | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Carburizing** | < 1:1 (excess acetylene) | ~2900 °C | Hardfacing, carburizing |
| **Neutral** | 1:1 | **3160 °C** | Most steels, general work |
| **Oxidizing** | > 1.5:1 (excess oxygen) | ~3300 °C | Brass, bronze welding |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Warning:** Students often confuse *carburizing* and *neutral* flame parameters. Memorize the **1:1 ratio** as the neutral flame reference. This is a high-yield 3-mark question topic.

---

## 2.2 Arc Welding Safety — Hazard Analysis

### 2.2.1 Electrical Hazards

Arc welding uses low voltage but **high current** (typical **20–500 A**). The open-circuit voltage (OCV) of an arc welding machine is **60–80 V DC**, but the arc voltage itself is **20–30 V**.

**Threshold Safety Values:**

$$I_{\text{let-go}} = 10 \text{ mA} \quad (\text{maximum current a person can release grip})$$

$$I_{\text{fibrillation}} = 100 \text{ mA} \quad (\text{ventricular fibrillation threshold at 50 Hz})$$

$$V_{\text{OCV}} = 60\text{–}80 \text{ V} \quad (\text{welding machine open-circuit voltage, DC})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** Although DC voltage at 80V is technically below the human "safe" threshold (50V AC / 120V DC per IS), welding operators work in **wet, sweaty conditions** and with **damaged gloves**, making even DC voltage extremely dangerous. **Never weld in the rain or standing in water.**

### 2.2.2 Radiation Hazards

The electric arc emits intense **Ultraviolet (UV), Visible, and Infrared (IR)** radiation. Exposure causes:

| Effect | Cause | Wavelength | Onset |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Arc Eye (Photokeratitis)** | UV-B, UV-C | 280–315 nm | 4–12 hours post-exposure |
| **Skin Burn (Welder's Flash)** | UV-A | 315–400 nm | Immediate |
| **Retinal Damage** | Visible, IR | 400–1400 nm | Latent |
| **Cataracts (chronic)** | IR-B, IR-C | 1400 nm–1 mm | Years |

**Lens Shade Selection (AWS Z49.1 / IS 1179):**

| Current Range (A) | Minimum Shade Number |
|---|---|
| < 60 | Shade 7 |
| 60–160 | Shade 10 |
| 160–250 | Shade 12 |
| 250–550 | Shade 14 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Standard:** Always cite **AWS Z49.1** or **IS 1179:1967** for welding PPE shade selection in exam answers.

### 2.2.3 Fume and Gas Hazards

Welding fumes are classified as **Group 1 carcinogens** by IARC (International Agency for Research on Cancer), specifically stainless steel welding fumes containing hexavalent chromium (Cr-VI).

**Threshold Limit Values (TLV — ACGIH):**

| Substance | TLV-TWA (mg/m³) | Health Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Manganese (Mn)** | **0.1** | Parkinsonian syndrome (CNS damage) |
| **Chromium VI (Cr-VI)** | **0.005** | Lung cancer |
| **Iron Oxide (Fe₂O₃)** | **5.0** | Siderosis |
| **Ozone (O₃)** | **0.05 ppm** | Pulmonary edema |
| **NOx (as NO₂)** | **0.5 ppm** | Bronchitis |
| **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** | **25 ppm** | Asphyxiation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mn-welding fume exposure is the single most important occupational health issue in welding.** Chronic exposure causes **manganism**, an irreversible Parkinsonian-like neurological disease. KTU frequently tests this fact.

### 2.2.4 Fire Prevention — The "Fire Triangle" Applied to Welding

Welding safety fundamentally defeats the **fire triangle**:

$$\text{Fire} = \text{Fuel} + \text{Oxygen} + \text{Heat (Ignition Source)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Hot Work Permit System (Mandatory per IS 3016 / NFPA 51B):**
> 1. **Hot Work Permit** issued by safety officer before work begins
> 2. **Fire Watch** posted within 11 meters (35 ft) of work — *never the welder themselves*
> 3. **Combustibles removed** within 11 m radius, or covered with fire-resistant blankets
> 4. **Fire extinguisher** (Class ABC dry powder / CO₂) within 3 m of work area
> 5. **Post-work inspection** for **at least 4 hours** after completion (smoldering fires)

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Oxy-Acetylene combustion | $2C_2H_2 + 5O_2 \rightarrow 4CO_2 + 2H_2O$ | Neutral flame heat release |
| 2 | Neutral flame ratio | $O_2 : C_2H_2 = 1 : 1$ | Optimal welding flame |
| 3 | Neutral flame temperature | $T_{neutral} = 3160^\circ C$ | Heat of combustion |
| 4 | Oxidizing flame ratio | $O_2 : C_2H_2 > 1.5 : 1$ | Brass/bronze welding |
| 5 | Acetylene decomposition limit | $T < 200^\circ C$ | Cylinder storage safety |
| 6 | Oxygen cylinder pressure | $P_{O_2} = 150 \text{ bar}$ | Standard full cylinder |
| 7 | Acetylene cylinder pressure | $P_{C_2H_2} = 1.5 \text{ bar}$ | Dissolved in acetone |
| 8 | Arc welding OCV | $V_{OC} = 60\text{–}80 \text{ V DC}$ | No-load voltage |
| 9 | Arc voltage (working) | $V_{arc} = 20\text{–}30 \text{ V}$ | During welding |
| 10 | Let-go threshold current | $I_{let\text{-}go} = 10 \text{ mA}$ | Human physiological limit |
| 11 | Fibrillation threshold | $I_{fib} = 100 \text{ mA}$ | Lethal AC current |
| 12 | Auto-ignition (oxygen + oil) | $T_{auto} \approx 250^\circ C$ | Oxygen line contamination |
| 13 | Cylinder segregation distance | $d \geq 6 \text{ m}$ OR firewall $h \geq 1.5 \text{ m}$ | Storage safety |
| 14 | Fire watch radius | $r \geq 11 \text{ m}$ | Hot work safety perimeter |
| 15 | Mn fume TLV | $TLV_{Mn} = 0.1 \text{ mg/m}^3$ | Ventilation design |
| 16 | Cr-VI TLV | $TLV_{Cr\text{-}VI} = 0.005 \text{ mg/m}^3$ | Carcinogen control |
| 17 | Lens shade selection | Current $\uparrow \Rightarrow$ Shade $\uparrow$ | PPE specification |
| 18 | Local exhaust velocity | $v_{capture} = 0.5\text{–}1.0 \text{ m/s}$ | LEV design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Memorize formulas #2, #3, #6, #7, #8, #10, #13, #14. These appear in **almost every KTU exam** related to this module.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Oxygen Requirement for Neutral Flame

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the volumetric oxygen requirement to produce a perfectly neutral oxy-acetylene flame, given the stoichiometric reaction and Avogadro's Law.

### Step 1 — Write the Balanced Combustion Equation

For complete combustion of acetylene (C₂H₂):

$$2C_2H_2 + 5O_2 \rightarrow 4CO_2 + 2H_2O + \Delta H$$

Where $\Delta H \approx -1300 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (highly exothermic).

### Step 2 — Apply Stoichiometric Mole Ratio

From the balanced equation, the mole ratio of oxygen to acetylene is:

$$\frac{n_{O_2}}{n_{C_2H_2}} = \frac{5}{2} = 2.5$$

This is the **stoichiometric ratio for complete combustion in pure oxygen**.

### Step 3 — Adjust for the Welding Torch Reality

In a welding torch, we use **pure oxygen** (not air). However, in practice, a slight excess of acetylene (about **10%**) is preferred to produce a **slightly reducing flame** that protects the weld pool from oxidation. This is the **"neutral flame"** in welding terminology (slightly carburizing).

$$\text{Neutral Flame: } \frac{O_2}{C_2H_2} = \frac{2.5}{1} \times (1 - 0.10) \approx 1:1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on terminology:** *Combustion-neutral* (pure stoichiometric) is 2.5:1, but *welding-neutral* (the practical standard taught in KTU) is **1:1 by volume** delivered at the torch.

### Step 4 — Convert Mole Ratio to Volume Ratio

By **Avogadro's Law**, equal moles of gases occupy equal volumes at the same temperature and pressure:

$$\frac{V_{O_2}}{V_{C_2H_2}} = \frac{n_{O_2}}{n_{C_2H_2}} = 1:1$$

Therefore, at the torch inlet:

$$\boxed{V_{O_2} : V_{C_2H_2} = 1 : 1}$$

### Step 5 — Calculate Heat Released Per Unit Volume

Heat of combustion per mole of acetylene:

$$Q = 1300 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

Volume of 1 mole at STP = 22.4 L. So per liter of acetylene:

$$q = \frac{1300 \text{ kJ}}{22.4 \text{ L}} = 58.04 \text{ kJ/L}$$

$$\boxed{q_{C_2H_2} \approx 58 \text{ kJ/L}}$$

This explains why acetylene is the preferred fuel gas — it releases enormous energy per liter.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Flashback Velocity vs. Gas Velocity

**Concept:** Flashback occurs when the **flame propagation speed** exceeds the **gas flow velocity** in the hose/torch. To prevent flashback, gas velocity must always exceed flame speed.

**Flame speed of oxy-acetylene mixture:**

$$v_{flame} = 7.5 \text{ m/s} \quad (\text{at stoichiometric ratio in 6 mm hose})$$

**Safety design criterion:**

$$v_{gas} > v_{flame}$$

For a typical hose of inner diameter $d = 6 \text{ mm} = 0.006 \text{ m}$, the minimum volumetric flow rate $Q$ is:

$$Q = v_{gas} \times A = v_{gas} \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

Substituting:

$$Q = 7.5 \times \frac{\pi \times (0.006)^2}{4}$$

$$Q = 7.5 \times \frac{\pi \times 3.6 \times 10^{-5}}{4}$$

$$Q = 7.5 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$Q = 2.12 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{min} = 0.212 \text{ L/s} = 12.7 \text{ L/min}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Implication:** If your regulator setting drops acetylene flow below 12.7 L/min through a 6 mm hose, flashback risk becomes significant. This is why **flow gauges** and **flashback arrestors** are mandatory.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Safety Analysis

| Safety Dimension | Gas Welding Control | Arc Welding Control | Statutory Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source Isolation** | Cylinder valves + flashback arrestors | Disconnect switch, electrode holder insulation | IS 5571 |
| **Operator Protection** | Welding goggles (shade 4–6), leather gloves | Auto-darkening helmet (shade 10–14), leathers | IS 1179, ANSI Z87.1 |
| **Ventilation** | Natural ventilation (low fume) | LEV at 0.5–1.0 m/s capture velocity | IS 3103, ACGIH |
| **Fire Prevention** | No combustibles within 11 m | Hot Work Permit + Fire Watch | NFPA 51B, IS 3016 |
| **Electrical Safety** | N/A (non-electrical process) | Double-insulated cables, ELCB | IS 732, IEEE 80 |
| **Gas Cylinder Safety** | Chain upright, segregate 6 m | N/A (some shielding gases) | IS 3196 |
| **Confined Space** | Continuous gas monitoring (O₂ > 19.5%) | LEV + supplied air respirator | IS 14702, OSHA 1910.146 |
| **Training/Certification** | Welder competency certificate | Welding operator license (ASME Sec IX) | IS 7318 |
| **First Aid** | Burn kit + eyewash station | Burn kit + CPR-trained person | Factories Act Sec 45 |
| **Emergency Stop** | Cylinder shut-off valves | Welding machine E-stop button | IS 5571 |

---

## 3.4 Hazard Control Hierarchy (Applied to Welding)

The **Hierarchy of Controls** (most effective to least effective) applied to welding hazards:

$$\text{Elimination} > \text{Substitution} > \text{Engineering Control} > \text{Administrative Control} > \text{PPE}$$

**Example Application — Mn Fume Hazard:**

1. **Elimination:** Eliminate Mn from welding consumables (rare — affects weld quality)
2. **Substitution:** Use low-Mn electrodes (e.g., E7018 instead of E6010)
3. **Engineering Control:** Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) with capture velocity 0.5 m/s
4. **Administrative Control:** Rotate workers, limit exposure time, medical surveillance
5. **PPE:** Powered Air Purifying Respirator (PAPR) with P100 filter

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Oxy-Acetylene Welding System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GasBank["Gas Storage Area"]
        OX["Oxygen Cylinder\n150 bar, Blue"]
        AC["Acetylene Cylinder\n1.5 bar, Red\nDissolved in Acetone"]
    end

    subgraph RegulatorStation["Regulator Station"]
        R1["Oxygen Regulator\nTwo-stage, 0-10 bar"]
        R2["Acetylene Regulator\nTwo-stage, 0-1.5 bar"]
    end

    subgraph SafetyDevices["Safety Devices"]
        FA1["Flashback Arrestor 1\nAt Regulator Outlet"]
        FA2["Flashback Arrestor 2\nAt Torch Inlet"]
        NV1["Hose Check Valve\nOxygen Line"]
        NV2["Hose Check Valve\nFuel Line"]
    end

    subgraph TorchAssembly["Welding Torch"]
        HANDLE["Torch Handle\nwith Oxygen\nand Fuel Valves"]
        TIP["Nozzle Tip\nSizes 1-7"]
        MIX["Mixing Chamber"]
    end

    FLAME["Neutral Flame\n3160 deg C\nO2:C2H2 = 1:1"]

    OX --> R1 --> FA1 --> NV1 --> HANDLE
    AC --> R2 --> FA2 --> NV2 --> HANDLE
    HANDLE --> MIX --> TIP --> FLAME
```

## 4.2 Arc Welding Circuit Architecture with Safety Controls

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PowerSource["Welding Power Source"]
        MAINS["3-Phase Mains Supply\n415V AC, 50 Hz"]
        XFORM["Step-down Transformer\n415V to 60-80V"]
        RECT["Rectifier\nAC to DC"]
        OCV["Open Circuit Voltage\n60-80 V DC"]
    end

    subgraph SafetyChain["Electrical Safety Chain"]
        ELCB["ELCB\n30 mA Trip"]
        MCB["MCB\n63 A Rated"]
        ESTOP["Emergency Stop\nMushroom Button"]
        SWITCH["Welding Switch\nMake-Before-Break"]
    end

    subgraph WeldingCircuit["Output Circuit"]
        ELEC["Electrode Holder\nInsulated to 1000V"]
        CABLE1["Welding Cable\nClass M, 50 sq mm"]
        WORK["Workpiece\nGrounded at Two Points"]
        ARC["Electric Arc\n8000 deg C"]
        CABLE2["Return Cable\nBonded to Workpiece"]
    end

    subgraph OperatorPPE["Operator PPE"]
        HELM["Auto-darkening Helmet\nShade 10-14"]
        APRON["Leather Apron\nFire Resistant"]
        GLOVES["Gauntlet Gloves\nHeat Resistant"]
        BOOTS["Safety Boots\nSteel Toe, EH Rated"]
    end

    MAINS --> MCB --> ESTOP --> XFORM --> RECT --> OCV --> SWITCH
    SWITCH --> ELEC
    ELEC --> CABLE1 --> ARC
    ARC --> WORK --> CABLE2 --> SWITCH
    ELCB -.Protective Earth.-> XFORM
```

## 4.3 Fire Triangle Application to Welding

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FireTriangle["Fire Triangle in Welding"]
        F["FUEL\nCylinder gases\nWorkplace combustibles\nCleaning solvents\nWood, paper, rags"]
        O["OXYGEN\nPure oxygen cylinders\nAtmospheric air 21 percent"]
        H["HEAT\nArc 8000 deg C\nFlame 3160 deg C\nSparks and spatter"]
    end

    FIRE["FIRE\nIndustrial Accident"]

    F --> FIRE
    O --> FIRE
    H --> FIRE

    subgraph Controls["Safety Controls Remove One Element"]
        C1["Remove Fuel\nClear 11 m radius\nNo flammable solvents"]
        C2["Isolate Oxygen\nCap cylinders\nVentilate confined spaces"]
        C3["Control Heat\nHot Work Permit\nFire Watch for 4 hours"]
    end

    C1 -.Removes.-> F
    C2 -.Removes.-> O
    C3 -.Removes.-> H
```

## 4.4 PPE Selection Decision Flow for Welding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Welding Task Identified"] --> Q1{"Process Type?"}

    Q1 -->|Gas Welding| GAS["Select Gas Welding PPE\nGoggles Shade 4-6\nLeather Gloves\nApron\nClosed Shoes"]
    Q1 -->|Arc Welding| Q2{"Current Range?"}

    Q2 -->|"Less than 60 A"| S7["Shade 7 Helmet\nLight Leather Gloves"]
    Q2 -->|"60 to 160 A"| S10["Shade 10 Helmet\nStandard Leather Gloves"]

    Q2 -->|"250 to 550 A"| S14["Shade 14 Helmet\nGauntlet Gloves\nFull Body Leather Suit\nSpats"]

    GAS --> VENT["Check Ventilation\nNatural for Gas\nLEV for Arc above 200 A"]
    S7 --> VENT
    S10 --> VENT
    S12 --> VENT
    S14 --> VENT

    VENT --> Q3{"Confined Space?"}
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Flashback and Backfire in Gas Welding. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Backfire:** It is a momentary retreat of the flame into the nozzle tip of the welding torch, accompanied by a loud popping sound. The flame either extinguishes or reignites immediately. Backfire is usually caused by **incorrect gas pressures**, **overheated nozzle**, or **obstructed tip**. It is a low-severity event and the torch can be relit.

**Flashback:** It is the propagation of the flame *upstream* from the nozzle into the hose, mixing chamber, and potentially into the cylinder. Flashback is **highly dangerous** because it can ignite the entire hose system and cause cylinder explosion. Prevention requires:
- Use of **flashback arrestors** at both regulator outlet and torch inlet
- Maintaining gas pressures within recommended range
- Avoiding overheating of nozzle

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students confuse the two terms or omit the **direction of flame propagation**. Always specify that **flashback goes upstream (against gas flow)**, while backfire remains at the nozzle tip.

---

### Question 2: List Six Essential PPE Items Required for Arc Welding. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The six essential PPE items for arc welding operators are:

1. **Welding Helmet with Filter Lens** — Auto-darkening or fixed shade 10–14 (as per current) — protects eyes and face from UV/IR radiation, sparks, and spatter.

2. **Flame-Resistant Clothing** — Leather apron or welding jacket — protects torso from sparks, spatter, and radiant heat.

3. **Welding Gloves (Gauntlets)** — Heat-resistant leather, full cuff — protects hands and forearms from heat, UV, and electric shock.

4. **Safety Boots** — Steel toe, Electrical Hazard (EH) rated — protects feet from falling objects and electrical contact.

5. **Leggings / Spats** — Leather coverings over boot tops — protects lower legs from sparks and spatter falling onto boots.

6. **Respiratory Protection** — FFP2 / N95 mask or PAPR for fume-laden environments — protects lungs from Mn, Cr-VI, and ozone exposure.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a) Explain the safety precautions to be followed during gas welding operations with reference to cylinder storage, regulators, flashback arrestors, and hose connections. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Cylinder Storage Safety [2 Marks]:**

- Oxygen and acetylene cylinders must be stored in **separate locations** with a minimum distance of **6 meters** OR separated by a **non-combustible firewall of 1.5 m height with 30-minute fire rating**.
- Cylinders must be **secured upright with chains or straps** to prevent toppling.
- Storage area must be **cool (below 50 °C)**, **well-ventilated**, and **free from flammable materials**.
- **Valve protection caps** must remain fitted when cylinders are not in use.
- Cylinders must be **color-coded**: oxygen (blue), acetylene (maroon/red), as per **IS 3196**.

**2. Regulators Safety [2 Marks]:**

- Use only **two-stage regulators** for oxy-acetylene work. The first stage reduces cylinder pressure to an intermediate level; the second stage provides precise working pressure.
- Oxygen regulators must be **green-marked**, acetylene regulators must be **red-marked** — they are **NOT interchangeable**.
- Never stand in front of a regulator during opening.
- Open cylinder valves slowly and stand to the side.

**3. Flashback Arrestors [2 Marks]:**

- Install **two flashback arrestors** per setup — one at the **regulator outlet** (toroidal/flame trap type) and one at the **torch inlet** (resettable type).
- Flashback arrestors prevent flame propagation upstream into the regulator and cylinder.
- Test flashback arrestors regularly (typically every 6 months) and replace if defective.

**4. Hose Connections [1 Mark]:**

- Use color-coded hoses — **red for acetylene, blue for oxygen** (as per IS 447).
- Use **hose check valves** to prevent backflow.
- Inspect hoses for leaks before each use with **soapy water** (never use oil/grease on oxygen fittings).
- Maximum hose length should not exceed **10 meters** to prevent excessive pressure drop.

---

**Q. (b) Discuss in detail the electrical, radiation, and fume hazards in arc welding and the corresponding safety controls. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Electrical Hazards and Controls [2.5 Marks]:**

- The arc welding machine has an **open-circuit voltage of 60–80 V DC** and operates at **20–500 A**.
- Although DC voltage below 120 V is considered intrinsically safe per **IS 732**, the operator works in **hot, sweaty conditions** with possibly damaged gloves, making even DC voltage dangerous.
- **Controls:** Double-insulated electrode holder rated to **1000 V**; welding cables with Class M insulation; **ELCB (30 mA trip)** on the welding machine; dry working conditions; never weld in rain or standing water; proper grounding of return cable to workpiece.

**2. Radiation Hazards and Controls [2.5 Marks]:**

- The arc emits intense **UV (200–400 nm), visible (400–700 nm), and IR (700 nm–1 mm)** radiation.
- **Arc Eye (Photokeratitis)** is caused by UV-B/UV-C exposure with delayed onset of 4–12 hours — symptoms include pain, photophobia, and tearing.
- Chronic exposure causes **cataracts** from IR radiation.
- **Controls:** Auto-darkening helmet with shade **10–14** depending on current (AWS Z49.1); leather clothing covering all exposed skin; arc-curtain screens around the workstation to protect nearby workers; "look-out" warning signage.

**3. Fume Hazards and Controls [2 Marks]:**

- Arc welding produces **metal oxide fumes** containing Mn, Cr, Fe, Ni, plus gases like ozone and NOx.
- **Manganese (Mn) exposure** causes **manganism** — an irreversible Parkinsonian syndrome. TLV = **0.1 mg/m³**.
- **Hexavalent Chromium (Cr-VI)** from stainless steel welding is a **Group 1 carcinogen**. TLV = **0.005 mg/m³**.
- **Controls:** **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** with capture velocity of **0.5–1.0 m/s** positioned within 30 cm of the arc; general ventilation of 10–20 air changes per hour; **P100/PAPR respirators** for high-exposure tasks; medical surveillance including baseline and periodic lung function tests and Mn blood/urine monitoring.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a) With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of an oxy-acetylene welding setup. Identify FIVE safety devices used in this process. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Diagram (Schematic Description):** [2 Marks]

The oxy-acetylene welding setup consists of:
- Two cylinders (oxygen-blue, acetylene-maroon) secured upright
- Two pressure regulators (oxygen & acetylene) with pressure gauges
- Two flashback arrestors at regulator outlets
- Two flexible hoses (red-acetylene, blue-oxygen)
- Two hose check valves
- A welding torch with handle, oxygen valve, fuel valve, mixing chamber, and interchangeable nozzle tips
- The flame produced at the nozzle tip (~3160 °C) melts and joins the metal pieces

**Safety Devices (Any Five) [5 Marks — 1 Mark Each]:**

1. **Two-Stage Pressure Regulators** — Reduce cylinder pressure to safe working pressure and maintain it constant.

2. **Flashback Arrestors** — Flame trap devices containing sintered metal element that quenches flame propagation. Installed at regulator and torch.

3. **Hose Check Valves** — Prevent reverse flow of gases into wrong line or cylinder.

4. **Non-Return Valve in Torch** — Prevents oxygen from flowing back into acetylene line.

5. **Pressure Relief Valves** — On acetylene regulators to release excess pressure if blocked.

6. **Flashback Arrestor with Dry Powder** — Some types use powder to absorb heat from flashback.

7. **Color-Coded Hoses** — Red for acetylene, blue for oxygen — prevents cross-connection.

---

**Q. (b) What is a Hot Work Permit? Explain the role of Fire Watch in welding operations with reference to relevant BIS/IS standards. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Hot Work Permit [3 Marks]:**

A **Hot Work Permit** is a written authorization issued by the **safety officer or competent person** before commencing any welding, cutting, brazing, or soldering operation that could ignite a fire. It is mandated under:
- **IS 3016** (Code of Practice for Fire Safety in Welding and Cutting)
- **NFPA 51B** (Standard for Fire Prevention During Welding, Cutting, and Other Hot Work)

**Contents of a Hot Work Permit:**
- Date, time, location of work
- Description of work to be done
- Identification of nearby combustibles and hazards
- Precautions taken (combustibles removed, area wetted, fire-resistant blankets used)
- Name of the welder and fire watch
- Validity period (typically 8 hours, renewable)
- Authorization signature

**2. Role of Fire Watch [4 Marks]:**

A **Fire Watch** is a trained person assigned to monitor the welding area for fire during and after the work. The fire watch responsibilities are:

- **Position:** Posted within **11 meters (35 feet)** of the work area, with full visibility of the operation.
- **Equipment:** Equipped with an appropriate **Class ABC fire extinguisher** (typically dry powder or CO₂), and means to raise the alarm.
- **Duration:** Must remain on duty **during the entire hot work** AND for **at least 4 hours after completion** to detect smoldering fires.
- **Tasks:**
  - Watch for sparks and spatter landing beyond the immediate area
  - Monitor cracks, holes, walls, and floors where sparks can travel
  - Test the area with thermal imaging if available
  - Extinguish incipient fires immediately
  - Sound alarm and evacuate if fire cannot be controlled
- **Cannot be the welder** — the fire watch must be a *dedicated* person with no other duties.
- **Compliance:** Per **Factories Act 1948 Section 38** and **IS 3016**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Students often write *"fire extinguisher"* without specifying the **class (ABC)** and **type (dry powder/CO₂)**. Water extinguishers are **NOT** suitable for electrical fires.
> 2. Students frequently forget the **4-hour post-work watch period**. This is a common marks-loss area.
> 3. The **fire watch cannot be the welder** — students often write "the welder must monitor" which is incorrect.
> 4. Always cite the **BIS/IS standard number** (e.g., IS 3016) — students lose 1 mark for omitting the standard reference.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Welding Safety**

### Gas Welding Safety Essentials
- Neutral flame: **O₂ : C₂H₂ = 1 : 1** by volume at **3160 °C**
- Combustion reaction: **2C₂H₂ + 5O₂ → 4CO₂ + 2H₂O**
- Cylinder pressures: Oxygen **150 bar**, Acetylene **1.5 bar** (dissolved in acetone)
- Cylinder segregation: **≥ 6 m** OR firewall **≥ 1.5 m height, 30-min fire rating**
- Acetylene decomposition temperature: **< 200 °C** (storage limit)
- Auto-ignition of oil + oxygen: **~250 °C** — *never use oil on oxygen fittings*
- Color codes: **Oxygen = Blue, Acetylene = Red/Maroon**
- Hose codes: **Oxygen = Blue/Black, Acetylene = Red**
- Two flashback arrestors mandatory: **at regulator + at torch**
- **Backfire** = nozzle tip only (low severity); **Flashback** = upstream propagation (high severity)

### Arc Welding Safety Essentials
- Open-circuit voltage: **60–80 V DC**; Arc voltage: **20–30 V**
- Let-go current: **10 mA**; Fibrillation: **100 mA** (50 Hz)
- Arc temperature: **6000–8000 °C**
- Lens shade selection: **Current ↑ → Shade ↑** (Shade 7 to 14 per AWS Z49.1)
- Critical fumes: **Mn (TLV 0.1 mg/m³)** causes manganism; **Cr-VI (TLV 0.005 mg/m³)** is carcinogenic
- LEV capture velocity: **0.5–1.0 m/s** within 30 cm of arc
- Arc Eye (Photokeratitis): UV-B/C, **delayed 4–12 hours**

### Universal Welding Safety Rules
- **Hot Work Permit** mandatory per **IS 3016 / NFPA 51B**
- **Fire Watch** posted within **11 m**, never the welder themselves
- **4-hour post-work fire watch** required
- **Class ABC fire extinguisher** within 3 m of work
- **PPE hierarchy:** Auto-darkening helmet → leather clothing → gauntlet gloves → EH boots → respirator

### Standards to Memorize for KTU
- **IS 3016** — Fire safety in welding
- **IS 1179** — Welding PPE shade selection
- **IS 447** — Hoses for gas welding
- **IS 3196** — Cylinder color coding
- **IS 732** — Electrical safety in welding
- **AWS Z49.1** — Safety in welding
- **NFPA 51B** — Fire prevention in hot work
- **Factories Act 1948** — Sections 38, 45 (fire safety, first aid)
- **ACGIH TLV booklet** — Fume exposure limits

### Common Confusions Clarified
| Confusion | Clarification |
|---|---|
| Carburizing vs Neutral flame | Carburizing = excess acetylene (exothermic reducing); Neutral = 1:1 |
| Flashback vs Backfire | Flashback = upstream to hose/cylinder; Backfire = nozzle tip only |
| OCV vs Arc voltage | OCV = 60-80 V (no load); Arc voltage = 20-30 V (working) |
| DC "safe" voltage myth | 80 V DC is theoretically "safe" but dangerous in wet/sweaty conditions |
| Stoichiometric vs Welding neutral | Stoichiometric = 2.5:1; Welding neutral = 1:1 |

# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Cutting and Finishing

### Formal Definition
> **Cutting**: An industrial manufacturing process in which a workpiece is subjected to controlled material removal using tools (single-point, multi-point, abrasive) to obtain a desired geometry, tolerance, and surface finish. In KTU 2024 Scheme context, cutting refers to **mechanical metal cutting (machining)**, **thermal cutting (oxy-fuel, plasma, laser)** and **abrasive cutting (grinding, polishing)**.

> **Finishing**: The final set of operations performed on a workpiece to achieve the specified surface roughness (Ra value), dimensional accuracy, and aesthetic appeal — including grinding, buffing, polishing, lapping, honing, and super-finishing.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine sculpting a statue from a block of marble. **Rough cutting** is when you chop away large chunks to get the basic shape. **Finishing** is when you use fine sandpaper to make the surface smooth and shiny. In industry, the "marble" is a metal bar, and the "chisel/sandpaper" are machine tools running at thousands of RPM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Hazards in cutting and finishing are primarily **mechanical** (rotating parts, chips, entanglement), **physical** (noise, vibration, dust), and **chemical** (coolant mist, metal fumes). Always identify the hazard source before selecting PPE.

---

## 1.2 Gas Cylinders and Equipment

### Formal Definition
> **Gas Cylinders**: High-pressure, portable containers manufactured from seamless or welded steel (and sometimes aluminium alloys) used for storing compressed, liquefied, or dissolved industrial gases such as **oxygen (O₂)**, **acetylene (C₂H₂)**, **LPG**, **argon (Ar)**, **nitrogen (N₂)**, and **carbon dioxide (CO₂)**.

### Working Pressure Benchmarks (Bold Standard Metrics)
- **Oxygen cylinders**: ~**150 bar (≈ 2175 psi)** at 15 °C.
- **Acetylene cylinders**: ~**17 bar** (acetylene is dissolved in acetone inside a porous mass for safety).
- **LPG cylinders**: ~**7 bar** at 40 °C (liquefied under pressure).

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a gas cylinder as a **"stressed energy bank"** — it stores energy that is released rapidly when the valve is opened. Like a loaded spring, it is harmless if sealed, but dangerous if the seal is breached or the cylinder is heated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Colour Coding (IS 4379 / ISO 32)**: Oxygen → **Black body, white shoulder**; Acetylene → **Maroon body, white shoulder**; LPG → **Red body**; Nitrogen → **Black body, brown shoulder**; Argon → **French grey body, dark green shoulder**.

---

## 1.3 Heat Treatment

### Formal Definition
> **Heat Treatment**: A controlled process of heating and cooling metals/alloys in the solid state to alter their **microstructure** and hence their **mechanical properties** (hardness, tensile strength, ductility, toughness) without changing the product shape.

Common Heat Treatment Processes:
| Process | Purpose |
|---|---|
| Annealing | Softening, relieve stress |
| Normalizing | Refine grain structure |
| Hardening | Increase hardness |
| Tempering | Reduce brittleness after hardening |
| Quenching | Rapid cooling to trap hard phases |
| Case Hardening (Carburizing, Nitriding) | Hard surface, tough core |

### Conceptual Analogy
Heat treatment is like **baking and cooling a cake** — by controlling oven temperature and how fast you cool it, you change the texture. In metals, you change the crystal grain structure to make it harder or softer.

---

## 1.4 Furnaces and Salt Baths

### Formal Definition
> **Furnace**: An enclosed chamber designed to deliver controlled high temperatures (typically **300 °C – 1800 °C**) for metallurgical processes such as melting, heat treatment, sintering, and smelting.

> **Salt Bath Furnace**: A special furnace in which the heating medium is a **molten salt mixture** (e.g., NaNO₃, KNO₃, NaCl, BaCl₂, NaCN) maintained at the required process temperature. The workpiece is immersed in the molten salt for uniform and rapid heating.

### Conceptual Analogy
A salt bath furnace is like a **"hot cooking oil deep fryer"** for metals. The molten salt is the "oil" that cooks the metal evenly from all sides, giving a uniform temperature much faster than air.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Salt Bath Hazard Note**: Cyanide salts (NaCN, KCN) used in **liquid carburizing** are **extremely toxic**. Reaction with acids liberates deadly **HCN gas**. Always maintain alkaline pH > 12 in the bath.

---

## 1.5 Operations and Maintenance (O&M)

### Formal Definition
> **Operations and Maintenance**: The set of routine, preventive, predictive, and breakdown maintenance activities performed on furnaces, salt baths, and associated equipment to ensure **safety, reliability, productivity**, and **energy efficiency**.

### Conceptual Analogy
O&M is the **"annual health check-up + daily exercise"** for industrial equipment. Skipping it is like ignoring a car warning light — small problems become catastrophic failures.

---

## 1.6 Safety in Handling and Storage of Salts

### Formal Definition
> The systematic set of procedures, engineering controls, PPE, and administrative measures adopted while **receiving, transporting, storing, dispensing, and disposing** of industrial salts (especially cyanide, nitrate, chromate, and chloride salts).

> [!WARNING]
> **Cyanide Salts (NaCN/KCN)**: Lethal dose for humans is as low as **200–300 mg**. Never store with acids. Maintain locked storage, MSDS availability, and emergency antidotes (Hydroxocobalamin / Sodium Thiosulfate).

---

## 1.7 Disposal of Effluents

### Formal Definition
> **Effluent**: Any liquid or gaseous waste discharged from an industrial process. Disposal involves **physical**, **chemical**, and **biological** treatment before release into the environment, complying with statutory norms (e.g., **PCB/CPCB norms**, MoEFCC guidelines, GSR 422(E)).

---

## 1.8 Health Precautions

### Formal Definition
> **Health Precautions**: Medical and hygienic measures — pre-employment and periodic medical examinations, biological monitoring, vaccination, hygiene practices, and first-aid preparedness — to protect workers from occupational illnesses arising from heat, fumes, chemicals, and physical agents.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Deep Theoretical Breakdown — Cutting Operations

### 2.1.1 Mechanics of Metal Cutting
In orthogonal cutting, the **primary shear angle (φ)** is governed by Ernst-Merchant equation:

$$\tan \phi = \frac{r \cos \alpha}{1 - r \sin \alpha}$$

where $r$ is the **cutting ratio** = $\dfrac{t_1}{t_2}$ (uncut chip thickness ÷ cut chip thickness), and $\alpha$ is the **rake angle**.

### 2.1.2 Hazard Sources in Cutting
- **Mechanical**: Rotating spindles, chuck jaws, sharp chips (especially continuous long chips in mild steel).
- **Physical**: Noise (>85 dB), vibration (HAVS - Hand Arm Vibration Syndrome), metal dust.
- **Chemical**: Coolant mist (MWF – Metal Working Fluids can cause dermatitis, occupational asthma).
- **Thermal**: Hot chips can reach **600 °C – 800 °C**, capable of causing burns.

### 2.1.3 Safety Control Hierarchy
1. **Elimination** – Use CNC enclosed machines.
2. **Substitution** – Use water-based coolants instead of oils.
3. **Engineering Controls** – Chip conveyors, machine guards, local exhaust ventilation (LEV).
4. **Administrative** – Job rotation, training.
5. **PPE** – Safety goggles, gloves (NOT loose near rotating parts), earplugs, aprons.

---

## 2.2 Deep Theoretical Breakdown — Gas Cylinders

### 2.2.1 Gas Laws (Foundation)
**Ideal Gas Law**:

$$PV = nRT$$

where $P$ = pressure (Pa), $V$ = volume (m³), $n$ = moles, $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** (universal gas constant), $T$ = absolute temperature (K).

**Gay-Lussac's Law** (constant V):

$$\frac{P_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2}{T_2}$$

### 2.2.2 Hazards of Compressed Gases
| Hazard | Cause | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Cylinder explosion** | Heat exposure → pressure rise | Cylinder rocketing (BLEVE if LPG) |
| **Oxygen enrichment** | Leak in confined space | Fire, clothing ignition |
| **Acetylene decomposition** | Pressure > 2 bar abs. unconfined | Explosive decomposition to C + H₂ |
| **Asphyxiation** | N₂, CO₂, Ar leak in pit | Loss of consciousness |
| **Backfire / Flashback** | Wrong regulator on O₂ + fuel | Torch explosion |

### 2.2.3 Acetylene Safety Rule
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Acetylene is **never** used above **1.03 bar (15 psi)** working pressure at the torch. Above this, it self-decomposes exothermically into Carbon and Hydrogen — the cylinder can detonate. This is why acetylene cylinders contain **acetone + porous mass**.

---

## 2.3 Deep Theoretical Breakdown — Heat Treatment

### 2.3.1 Phase Transformation Concept
Heating steel above the **upper critical temperature (A₃ or Acm)** transforms **Ferrite + Cementite → Austenite (γ-Fe)**. On cooling, austenite transforms to martensite, pearlite, bainite, or ferrite depending on cooling rate.

### 2.3.2 TTT Diagram and CCT Diagram
- **TTT (Time-Temperature-Transformation)** diagram shows isothermal transformation kinetics.
- **CCT (Continuous Cooling Transformation)** diagram is more practical for industrial cooling.

### 2.3.3 Hardenability — Grossmann's Equation

$$D_I = D_Q \times HF$$

where $D_I$ = ideal critical diameter of a given steel, $D_Q$ = critical diameter for the quenching medium (e.g., water, oil), and $HF$ = hardenability factor (depends on alloy content).

### 2.3.4 Heat Required for Heating Workpiece

$$Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

where $Q$ = heat energy (J), $m$ = mass (kg), $c_p$ = specific heat capacity (J/kg·K) of steel ≈ **460 J/(kg·K)**, $\Delta T$ = temperature rise (K).

For furnaces, additional heat for walls, fixtures, and losses:

$$Q_{total} = \frac{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + Q_{losses}}{\eta_{furnace}}$$

where $\eta_{furnace}$ = thermal efficiency (typically **30 % – 60 %** for oil/gas furnaces).

### 2.3.5 Heat Treatment Hazards
- **Burns** from hot workpieces (steel at 850 °C radiates intensely).
- **Fire** from oil quench tanks (water contamination → steam explosion).
- **Fume inhalation** from molten salts, decarburizing atmospheres.
- **Explosion** from gas-fired furnaces (unburnt gas accumulation).

---

## 2.4 Deep Theoretical Breakdown — Furnaces and Salt Baths

### 2.4.1 Types of Furnaces
- **Crucible furnace** – for non-ferrous melting.
- **Reverberatory furnace** – large scale melting (steel, copper).
- **Muffle furnace** – heating chamber isolated from combustion gases (clean atmosphere).
- **Induction furnace** – electromagnetic induction heating.
- **Resistance furnace** – electric heating elements (NiCr, SiC, MoSi₂).
- **Vacuum furnace** – for aerospace/tool steels.

### 2.4.2 Salt Bath Composition by Temperature Range
| Salt Bath Type | Composition | Operating Temperature |
|---|---|---|
| Low temperature | NaNO₃ + KNO₃ (50:50) | **150 °C – 550 °C** |
| Medium temperature | NaCl + KCl (50:50) | **700 °C – 900 °C** |
| High temperature | BaCl₂ + NaCl | **950 °C – 1300 °C** |
| Cyanide bath | NaCN + BaCl₂ (carburizing) | **800 °C – 950 °C** |
| Neutral / Oxidizing | Na₂CO₃ + NaCl | **750 °C – 900 °C** |

### 2.4.3 Salt Bath Heat Transfer

Heat transfer coefficient in molten salt:

$$h = 1500 - 5000 \quad W/(m^2 \cdot K)$$

(compared to air furnace $h \approx 50 - 300$ $W/(m^2 K)$). This explains **20–100× faster** heating rates in salt baths.

### 2.4.4 Salt Bath Reactions — Cyanide Case
In **liquid carburizing (cyaniding)**:
$$2NaCN + O_2 \rightarrow 2NaCNO$$
$$2NaCNO + O_2 \rightarrow Na_2CO_3 + CO + 2N$$
$$3Fe + 2CO \rightarrow Fe_3C + CO_2$$
$$Fe + 2N \rightarrow Fe_2N \,(\text{nitrides})$$

> [!WARNING]
> Acid contact with cyanide salts liberates **HCN gas** — lethal within minutes. Always store cyanide salts in **alkaline, dry, locked** conditions. Never co-store with acids or acidic salts.

---

## 2.5 Operations and Maintenance

### 2.5.1 Types of Maintenance
- **Breakdown (Reactive) Maintenance**: Fix after failure.
- **Preventive Maintenance (PM)**: Scheduled inspections (e.g., refractory thickness check).
- **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)**: Condition monitoring (vibration analysis, IR thermography).
- **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)**: Japanese model — operator-driven autonomous maintenance.

### 2.5.2 Furnace O&M Critical Tasks
- Daily: Check burner flame, thermocouple reading, salt level.
- Weekly: Inspect refractory cracks, door seals.
- Monthly: Calibrate temperature controllers, clean burners.
- Quarterly: Check structural integrity, gas leak test.
- Annual: Refractory relining (if wear > 30 %).

### 2.5.3 Permits for Maintenance
- **Hot Work Permit** – welding/cutting near flammable areas.
- **Confined Space Entry Permit** – inside furnaces, tanks.
- **Lockout-Tagout (LOTO)** – energy isolation before maintenance.

---

## 2.6 Safety in Handling and Storage of Salts

### 2.6.1 Hazard Classification of Common Salts
| Salt | Hazard | GHS Pictogram |
|---|---|---|
| NaCN / KCN | Acute toxicity (Oral, Dermal, Aquatic) | Skull & Crossbones, Environment |
| NaNO₃ / KNO₃ | Oxidizer (fire accelerator) | Flame over circle |
| Na₂Cr₂O₇ | Carcinogen, oxidizer | Health hazard, Skull |
| BaCl₂ | Toxic (muscle paralysis) | Skull |
| NaCl | Low hazard, hygroscopic | None |
| NaOH / KOH | Corrosive (skin/eyes) | Corrosion |

### 2.6.2 Storage Rules
- **Segregate** acids from cyanide and oxidizers (compatibility chart).
- **Ventilated, dry, locked** storage.
- **Bunded** floor (110 % of largest container capacity) to contain spills.
- **MSDS** at storage and use point.
- **Inventory control** with FIFO.

### 2.6.3 PPE for Salt Handling
- Chemical splash goggles.
- Face shield for powder handling.
- Nitrile/neoprene gloves (not latex).
- Respiratory protection (P3 / N100 mask for dust).
- Apron / chemical-resistant coveralls.

---

## 2.7 Disposal of Effluents

### 2.7.1 Effluent Treatment Stages
1. **Pre-treatment** – Oil removal, equalization.
2. **Primary** – Sedimentation, flotation.
3. **Secondary** – Biological (activated sludge, biofilm).
4. **Tertiary** – Filtration, activated carbon, ion exchange, RO.
5. **Sludge treatment** – Thickening, dewatering, disposal.

### 2.7.2 Cyanide Effluent Destruction
**Alkaline Chlorination Process**:

$$\text{NaCN} + 2\text{NaOH} + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{NaCNO} + 2\text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
$$2\text{NaCNO} + 4\text{NaOH} + 3\text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + \text{N}_2 + 6\text{NaCl} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Final pH must be **8.5 – 9.5** and residual chlorine **< 1 mg/L** before discharge.

### 2.7.3 Statutory Limits for Discharge
| Parameter | Inland Surface Water | Public Sewers |
|---|---|---|
| pH | **5.5 – 9.0** | 5.5 – 9.0 |
| Cyanide (as CN) | **0.2 mg/L** | 2 mg/L |
| Chromium (VI) | **0.1 mg/L** | 2 mg/L |
| Total Suspended Solids | 100 mg/L | 600 mg/L |
| Oil & Grease | 10 mg/L | 20 mg/L |

### 2.7.4 pH Calculation (Basics)
$$\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$$

For strong base dilution:
$$[\text{OH}^-] = \frac{C_b V_b}{V_{total}} \quad \text{(before equivalence)}$$

---

## 2.8 Health Precautions

### 2.8.1 Occupational Health Hazards in Heat Treatment
- **Heat stress** (heat exhaustion, heat stroke).
- **Respiratory irritation** from salt fumes, oil mist.
- **Dermatitis** from coolant oils.
- **Cataract** from IR radiation in furnaces.
- **Cyanide poisoning** (acute/chronic).

### 2.8.2 Biological Monitoring
- **Cyanide workers**: Urinary thiocyanate (SCN⁻) — BTL **< 50 mg/g creatinine**.
- **Chromium workers**: Urinary chromium.
- **Lead workers**: Blood lead level.

### 2.8.3 First-Aid for Specific Exposures
| Exposure | First Aid |
|---|---|
| Cyanide inhalation | Move to fresh air, 100 % O₂, amyl nitrite, hydroxocobalamin IV |
| Acid/alkali splash | 15 min eyewash, do not neutralize on skin |
| Heat burn | Cool 20 min under running water, cover with sterile dressing |
| Heat stroke | Cool body, IV fluids, medical emergency |

---

## 2.9 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA / CHEAT SHEET

| Topic | Formula / Equation | Variables | Standard Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ideal Gas Law | $PV = nRT$ | P, V, n, T | R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) |
| Gay-Lussac | $\dfrac{P_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{T_2}$ | Pressure vs. Temp | Constant V |
| Heat Required | $Q = m c_p \Delta T$ | Q, m, cp, ΔT | cp (steel) = 460 J/(kg·K) |
| Furnace Heat Balance | $Q_{total} = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{\eta}$ | Q, η | η = 30–60 % |
| Grossmann Hardenability | $D_I = D_Q \times HF$ | DI, DQ, HF | Depends on alloy |
| Chip Thickness Ratio | $r = \dfrac{t_1}{t_2}$ | Uncut / cut chip | r > 1 always |
| pH | $\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$ | [H⁺] in mol/L | Pure water = 7 |
| Power for Furnace | $P = \dfrac{Q}{t} = \dfrac{VI \cos\phi}{1000}$ | kW | — |
| Stress in Cylinder Wall | $\sigma = \dfrac{Pd}{2t}$ (thin wall) | Hoop stress | σ < σ_allow |
| Cyanide Detox Stoichiometry | 2 CN⁻ + 5 Cl₂ + 8 OH⁻ → 2 CO₃²⁻ + N₂ + 10 Cl⁻ + 4 H₂O | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> Always keep critical **KtU 2024 numbers** in mind: Acetylene working pressure ≤ 1.03 bar, cyaniding temperature 800–950 °C, salt bath heating coefficient 1500–5000 W/(m²·K), CN⁻ TLV = **5 mg/m³** (ACGIH).

# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivative 1 — Pressure Rise in a Gas Cylinder Exposed to Heat

**Problem**: An oxygen cylinder at **150 bar** and **15 °C** (288.15 K) is accidentally exposed to a fire and its wall temperature rises to **200 °C** (473.15 K). Calculate the new internal pressure (assuming constant volume — sealed valve).

### Step-by-Step Solution

By **Gay-Lussac's Law** (constant V, n):

$$\frac{P_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2}{T_2}$$

Solving for $P_2$:

$$P_2 = P_1 \times \frac{T_2}{T_1}$$

Substituting values:

$$P_2 = 150 \text{ bar} \times \frac{473.15 \, \text{K}}{288.15 \, \text{K}}$$

$$P_2 = 150 \text{ bar} \times 1.6420$$

$$P_2 = 246.3 \, \text{bar}$$

### Step-by-Step Numeric Evaluation
- $T_1$ conversion: $15 + 273.15 = 288.15$ K.
- $T_2$ conversion: $200 + 273.15 = 473.15$ K.
- Temperature ratio: $473.15 / 288.15 = 1.6420$ (dimensionless).
- Final pressure: $150 \times 1.6420 = 246.30$ bar.

**Engineering Interpretation**: Test pressure of oxygen cylinders is typically **250 bar**. So the cylinder is on the verge of mechanical failure. This is why **all gas cylinders must be stored away from fire / heat sources** — heat exposure raises pressure catastrophically.

---

## 3.2 Derivative 2 — Heat Energy Required to Austenitize a Steel Workpiece

**Problem**: A carbon steel forging of mass **50 kg** is to be heat treated. Initial temperature **30 °C**, austenitizing temperature **850 °C**. Specific heat capacity $c_p = 460$ J/(kg·K). Furnace efficiency $\eta = 40$ %. Calculate the actual energy input from the burner.

### Step-by-Step Solution

Step 1 — Compute the temperature rise:

$$\Delta T = T_{final} - T_{initial} = 850 - 30 = 820 \, \text{K}$$

Step 2 — Compute useful heat stored in the workpiece:

$$Q_{useful} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

$$Q_{useful} = 50 \times 460 \times 820$$

Step 3 — Multiply out:

$$Q_{useful} = 50 \times 460 = 23{,}000 \quad \text{(J/K)}$$

$$Q_{useful} = 23{,}000 \times 820 = 18{,}860{,}000 \, \text{J}$$

$$Q_{useful} = 1.886 \times 10^7 \, \text{J} = 18.86 \, \text{MJ}$$

Step 4 — Adjust for furnace efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{input}}$$

$$Q_{input} = \frac{Q_{useful}}{\eta} = \frac{18.86 \, \text{MJ}}{0.40}$$

$$Q_{input} = 47.15 \, \text{MJ}$$

### Engineering Interpretation
The actual fuel input is **2.5×** the useful heat. This 60 % loss is due to flue gas exhaust, wall radiation, door openings, and water vapor. Modern recuperative furnaces recover part of this, achieving efficiencies of **60–70 %**.

---

## 3.3 Derivative 3 — Cooling Rate and Hardness Selection

**Problem**: A 0.4 % C steel cylinder (mass 10 kg, surface area $A = 0.05$ m²) is quenched in oil. The surface heat transfer coefficient $h = 1500$ W/(m²·K). Bulk oil temp $T_\infty = 60$ °C, surface temp $T_s = 800$ °C. Calculate the instantaneous heat extraction rate.

### Step-by-Step Solution

Using Newton's Law of Cooling:

$$\dot{Q} = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$$

Substituting:

$$\dot{Q} = 1500 \times 0.05 \times (800 - 60)$$

$$\dot{Q} = 1500 \times 0.05 = 75 \quad \text{(W/K)}$$

$$\dot{Q} = 75 \times 740$$

$$\dot{Q} = 55{,}500 \, \text{W} = 55.5 \, \text{kW}$$

### Interpretation
A **55.5 kW** heat flux is removed in initial seconds — this is why oil quench tanks need:
- **Agitation** (to maintain h).
- **Cooling coils** (to absorb heat).
- **Fire extinguishers** (oil temp > flash point = **130 °C** for mineral quench oil).

---

## 3.4 Derivative 4 — Stoichiometric Air Required for Gas Combustion

**Problem**: Natural gas (assume pure CH₄) is combusted in a furnace. Calculate the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (mass basis).

**Combustion reaction**:

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

### Step-by-Step Solution

Step 1 — Molar mass balance:
- 1 mole CH₄ = **16 g**.
- 2 moles O₂ = 2 × 32 = **64 g**.
- Air contains 21 % O₂ and 79 % N₂ by volume, so per mole O₂ → $\dfrac{79}{21} = 3.762$ moles N₂.
- Mass of air = mass of O₂ + mass of N₂ = $64 + (2 \times 3.762 \times 28)$ = $64 + 210.7$ = **274.7 g**.

Step 2 — Mass air / mass fuel ratio:

$$\text{A/F} = \frac{274.7}{16} = 17.17$$

Step 3 — In practice, 10–25 % excess air is supplied:

$$\text{A/F}_{actual} = 17.17 \times 1.15 = 19.75$$

### Interpretation
For **every kg of methane**, ~**19.75 kg of air** is supplied in furnace combustion. With insufficient air, **CO (carbon monoxide)** forms — deadly invisible gas. Furnace flue must be tested for **CO < 50 ppm**.

---

## 3.5 Derivative 5 — Concentration of Free Cyanide in Salt Bath Effluent

**Problem**: A salt bath contains 100 L of spent quench water with [CN⁻] = 200 mg/L. Alkaline chlorination is used to destroy it. Calculate the mass of Cl₂ required for complete oxidation.

**Reaction** (complete oxidation):

$$\text{CN}^- + \frac{5}{2}\text{Cl}_2 + 4\text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{CO}_3^{2-} + \text{N}_2 + 5\text{Cl}^- + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

### Step-by-Step Solution

Step 1 — Total CN⁻ mass in effluent:

$$m_{CN^-} = 200 \, \text{mg/L} \times 100 \, \text{L} = 20{,}000 \, \text{mg} = 20 \, \text{g}$$

Step 2 — Moles of CN⁻:

$$n_{CN^-} = \frac{20}{26} = 0.7692 \, \text{mol}$$

(where 26 g/mol = molar mass of CN⁻).

Step 3 — Moles of Cl₂ needed (stoichiometric):

$$n_{Cl_2} = \frac{5}{2} \times n_{CN^-} = 2.5 \times 0.7692 = 1.923 \, \text{mol}$$

Step 4 — Mass of Cl₂:

$$m_{Cl_2} = 1.923 \times 71 = 136.6 \, \text{g}$$

Step 5 — With 10 % excess:

$$m_{Cl_2, actual} = 136.6 \times 1.10 = 150.3 \, \text{g}$$

### Interpretation
Just **150 g** of chlorine destroys all cyanide from 100 L of 200 ppm effluent. The treated effluent must have:
- Residual Cl₂ < **1 mg/L** (dechlorinate with SO₂ if higher).
- pH **8.5 – 9.5** (residual alkalinity).
- Free CN⁻ < **0.2 mg/L** before discharge.

---

| Cylinder pressure rise | T₁, T₂, P₁ | P₂ | Gay-Lussac |
