# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: List any six hazards associated with gas cylinders and state the corresponding preventive measures.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
1. **Cylinder explosion** due to overpressure → *Preventive*: store below 50 °C, never roll/drop, install rupture disc.
2. **Oxygen enrichment fire** → *Preventive*: keep O₂ cylinders away from oil/grease, no smoking.
3. **Acetylene decomposition** → *Preventive*: use below 1.03 bar working pressure, never heat above 50 °C.
4. **Asphyxiation** (N₂, CO₂) → *Preventive*: monitor O₂ in confined space, ventilation.
5. **Backfire / flashback** → *Preventive*: use flashback arrestor on torch.
6. **Cylinder rocketing** (valve shears off) → *Preventive*: cap valve, secure upright with chain.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Why is salt bath preferred over air furnace for heat treatment of tool steels? Mention two hazards of cyanide salt bath.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
- **Why preferred**: 
  - Heat transfer coefficient is **20–100× higher** than air → faster, uniform heating.
  - Prevents scaling and decarburization.
  - Provides protective atmosphere.
- **Two hazards of cyanide bath**:
  1. **HCN gas liberation** if salt contacts acid → lethal.
  2. **Sodium cyanide ingestion / skin absorption** → central nervous system failure; dose 200–300 mg fatal.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU ESE pattern for Module 3, each Part B question carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### QUESTION A — CHOICE 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: (a) With a neat diagram, explain the working of a salt bath furnace. List five safety precautions during operation. (7 marks)**
**(b) Explain the principle of alkaline chlorination for cyanide effluent disposal with balanced chemical equations. What are the discharge norms for cyanide? (7 marks)**

### Model Answer (a) — Working of Salt Bath Furnace & Safety

A **salt bath furnace** is a metallurgical heating unit in which the heating medium is a **molten mixture of salts** held in a steel pot externally heated by electrodes (or gas burners).

**Working Principle:**
1. Solid salt mixture (e.g., NaCl + KCl for medium temp, NaCN + BaCl₂ for cyaniding) is loaded into a metal pot.
2. Electrodes immersed in salt pass **low-voltage high-amperage** AC current through the molten salt. Because of high electrical resistance of salt, **Joule heating ($I^2R$)** melts and maintains the salt at process temperature.
3. The **PID controller** with thermocouple feedback maintains bath temperature within ±5 °C.
4. Workpieces held in **tongs / fixtures** are lowered into the bath by hoist.
5. Heating is rapid and uniform; after the prescribed soaking time, the workpiece is lifted and quenched.

**Safety Precautions:**
1. **Pre-heat workpieces** to avoid salt splash.
2. Wear **face shield, leather gloves, leather apron**.
3. Maintain **exhaust hood** with LEV to extract salt fumes.
4. Keep **fire extinguisher (Class D)** for metal fires.
5. **Never quench wet workpieces** in molten salt — explosive spatter.
6. Maintain **emergency drain** to divert molten salt into a quench pit in case of pot failure.
7. **Lockout-tagout (LOTO)** for electrode maintenance.

### Model Answer (b) — Alkaline Chlorination of Cyanide

Cyanide in industrial effluent is destroyed by **oxidation with chlorine gas** under **strongly alkaline conditions** (pH > 11). It occurs in two stages:

**Stage 1 — Conversion of CN⁻ to CNO⁻ (cyanate):**

$$\text{NaCN} + 2\text{NaOH} + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{NaCNO} + 2\text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

This stage requires **pH ≥ 12** to prevent HCN formation. Reaction time 30–60 min.

**Stage 2 — Oxidation of cyanate to CO₂ and N₂:**

$$2\text{NaCNO} + 4\text{NaOH} + 3\text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + \text{N}_2 + 6\text{NaCl} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

This is done at **pH 8.5–9.5**. Reaction time 30 min.

**Net Reaction:**

$$2\text{CN}^- + 5\text{Cl}_2 + 8\text{OH}^- \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_3^{2-} + \text{N}_2 + 10\text{Cl}^- + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Discharge Norms for Cyanide (Indian CPCB — Inland Surface Water):**
| Parameter | Limit |
|---|---|
| **Total Cyanide (as CN)** | **0.2 mg/L** |
| **Free Cyanide** | **0.1 mg/L** |
| pH | **5.5 – 9.0** |

**Process Steps:**
1. Collect effluent in equalization tank.
2. Adjust pH > 12 with NaOH.
3. Dose Cl₂ (gas or NaOCl) per stoichiometry + 10 % excess.
4. Monitor ORP (Oxidation-Reduction Potential) ≥ +300 mV.
5. After 1 h, drop pH to 8.5–9.5.
6. Continue chlorination for 1 h.
7. Test residual CN⁻ and Cl₂; dechlorinate if Cl₂ > 1 mg/L using SO₂.
8. Discharge to ETP outlet.

---

### QUESTION B — CHOICE 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: (a) Explain with a flowchart the operations and maintenance procedure of a heat treatment furnace. (7 marks)**
**(b) Describe in detail the safety measures for handling and storage of cyanide salts. List four health effects of cyanide exposure. (7 marks)**

### Model Answer (a) — Operations and Maintenance of Heat Treatment Furnace

**Operations:**
- **Pre-start check**: Inspect refractory lining, check burner nozzles, thermocouple calibration, gas pressure, oil level in quench tank.
- **Loading**: Workpieces are pre-cleaned, loaded on trays / fixtures.
- **Heating**: Set temperature and ramp rate on PID controller. Soak time begins when set temperature is reached.
- **Soaking**: Hold at temperature for prescribed time (e.g., 30 min per 25 mm cross-section for steel).
- **Quenching**: Rapid transfer (< 5 s) to oil/water/polymer quench bath.
- **Tempering**: Reheat to 150–650 °C for stress relief.
- **Inspection**: Hardness testing, microstructure check.

**Maintenance:**
- **Daily**: Log furnace temperature profile, observe burner flame.
- **Weekly**: Inspect door seals, check thermocouple drift, clean salt spillage.
- **Monthly**: Calibrate instruments, inspect refractory for cracks.
- **Quarterly**: Combustion analysis, gas leak test, oil quenching bath cooling coil inspection.
- **Annual**: Refractory relining if wear > 30 %, burner overhaul.

**Predictive Tools**: Vibration analysis on fans, IR thermography for hot spots, ultrasonic thickness measurement of furnace shell.

**Permits**: LOTO for cold maintenance; Hot Work Permit for burner maintenance.

### Model Answer (b) — Cyanide Salt Safety

**Handling Safety:**
1. **MSDS** must be at every storage/use point.
2. Only **trained, certified personnel** handle cyanides.
3. Use **closed system / glove box** for weighing and dispensing.
4. Always work in **pairs (buddy system)** — never alone.
5. Wear **full PPE**: nitrile gloves, chemical splash goggles, face shield, dust mask (P3), full chemical-resistant suit.
6. Maintain **eyewash and emergency shower** within 10 m.
7. **Antidote kit** always available: Hydroxocobalamin injection, Amyl nitrite pearls, Sodium thiosulfate.

**Storage Safety:**
1. Store in **locked, ventilated, dry** room.
2. **Segregate** from acids (H₂SO₄, HCl, HNO₃), oxidizers, food.
3. Use **bunded floor** with 110 % containment.
4. Container must be **labelled** with **skull-and-crossbones pictogram**.
5. Maintain **inventory register** with batch, quantity, date of receipt.
6. **FIFO** stock rotation.
7. Store below **25 °C**, away from sunlight.

**Four Health Effects of Cyanide Exposure:**
1. **Respiratory failure** — HCN blocks cytochrome oxidase, halts cellular respiration.
2. **Cardiovascular collapse** — sudden BP drop, arrhythmia.
3. **CNS depression** — headache, confusion, coma, convulsions.
4. **Chronic thyroid dysfunction** — chronic exposure causes goitre due to thiocyanate accumulation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Reasons Students Lose Marks**
> 1. Writing **colour codes** of cylinders **wrong** — memorize IS 4379 table.
> 2. Confusing **hardening** with **tempering** — they are sequential, not alternate.
> 3. Forgetting the **two-stage** alkaline chlorination — examiners specifically look for **pH ≥ 12 first, then pH 8.5–9.5**.
> 4. Wrong **stoichiometry** of Cl₂ for CN⁻ destruction — the correct ratio is **2.5 mol Cl₂ per mol CN⁻**.
> 5. Confusing **fire extinguisher class** — molten salt / alkali metal fires need **Class D**, not water.
> 6. Not mentioning **Lockout-Tagout (LOTO)** for maintenance — must appear in any maintenance answer.
> 7. Writing **acetyleneworking pressure** > 1.03 bar — automatic 2-mark penalty.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### Cutting & Finishing
- [ ] Cutting hazards: rotating parts, chips (hot at 600 °C), noise, vibration.
- [ ] Hierarchy of controls: Eliminate → Substitute → Engineering → Admin → PPE.
- [ ] Coolant mist → dermatitis, occupational asthma → LEV mandatory.

### Gas Cylinders
- [ ] Oxygen = **150 bar**, Acetylene = **17 bar**, LPG = **7 bar**.
- [ ] Acetylene working pressure at torch ≤ **1.03 bar**.
- [ ] Store upright, capped, chained, away from heat (>50 °C).
- [ ] Colour coding as per IS 4379.
- [ ] Backfire arrestors on both torch and regulator lines.

### Heat Treatment
- [ ] Four primary processes: Anneal, Normalize, Harden, Temper.
- [ ] Austenitizing temperature for steel = **Ac₃ + 30–50 °C**.
- [ ] Quench severity: brine > water > oil > polymer > air.
- [ ] Hardness testing: Rockwell (HRC), Brinell (HB), Vickers (HV).

### Furnaces & Salt Baths
- [ ] Salt bath heating 20–100× faster than air furnace.
- [ ] Cyanide salt bath at **800–950 °C** for case hardening.
- [ ] Never heat wet workpieces — explosion risk.
- [ ] PID control with ±5 °C tolerance.
- [ ] Emergency drain to quench pit mandatory.

### Operations & Maintenance
- [ ] 4 types: Breakdown, Preventive, Predictive, TPM.
- [ ] Permits required: Hot Work, Confined Space, LOTO.
- [ ] Annual refractory inspection.

### Salt Handling & Storage
- [ ] Cyanide: Segregate from acids, locked storage, alkaline.
- [ ] Nitrate salts: oxidizer — keep away from flammables.
- [ ] MSDS available at all times.

### Effluent Disposal
- [ ] **2-stage alkaline chlorination** for cyanide.
- [ ] Stoichiometry: **2.5 mol Cl₂ per mol CN⁻**.
- [ ] Discharge limit CN⁻ < **0.2 mg/L** (surface water).
- [ ] pH of discharge: **5.5 – 9.0**.

### Health Precautions
- [ ] Pre-employment + annual medical exams.
- [ ] Biological monitoring: urinary thiocyanate < **50 mg/g creatinine**.
- [ ] First-aid: Amyl nitrite → IV Hydroxocobalamin.
- [ ] Heat stress prevention: hydration, rest cycles, acclimatization.

### KTU 2024 Frequently Tested Numbers
- [ ] R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)
- [ ] cp (steel) = 460 J/(kg·K)
- [ ] O₂ cylinder 150 bar; Acetylene ≤ 1.03 bar working
- [ ] Salt bath h = 1500–5000 W/(m²·K)
- [ ] Cyanide TLV = 5 mg/m³
- [ ] CN⁻ discharge limit = 0.2 mg/L
- [ ] Furnace efficiency = 30–60 %

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exposure to Hazardous Fumes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT834 - HAZARD CONTROL IN INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Exposure to Hazardous Fumes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **hazardous fume** is a finely divided solid particulate (typically < 1 μm aerodynamic diameter) generated by the condensation of vaporized materials during processes such as welding, soldering, smelting, melting, or thermal cutting. In industrial hygiene terminology, fumes are distinguished from **dusts** (mechanically generated solid particles), **mists** (liquid droplets), **gases** (substances in gaseous state at room temperature), and **vapors** (gaseous form of a substance normally liquid at room temperature).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "A fume is an aerosol of solid particles formed by the condensation of vaporized material, especially metals, frequently accompanied by chemical reaction with air. Hazardous fumes are airborne contaminants in workplaces that may cause acute or chronic adverse health effects via inhalation, skin absorption, or ingestion."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of hazardous fumes as an **invisible, floating dust cloud from a campfire**, but in a factory or workshop. When metal is heated to thousands of degrees, it vaporizes, and when that vapor cools, it forms ultra-fine solid particles that remain suspended in the air. Because they are so small, they don't simply fall to the ground — they **float, drift into your lungs, and some even cross into your bloodstream**, much like cigarette smoke particles entering the deep recesses of the lung.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Industry Examples:**
> - **Welding fumes** — iron, manganese, chromium from arc welding
> - **Lead fumes** — from soldering, battery manufacturing, smelting
> - **Zinc fumes** — from galvanizing operations ("metal fume fever")
> - **Polymer fumes** — from burning plastics (HF, HCl, dioxins)
> - **Solvents** — benzene, toluene, xylene vapors in paint shops

## 1.3 Key Distinctions (KTU Exam Favorites)

| Term | Physical State | Size Range | Example |
|------|---------------|------------|---------|
| Fume | Solid (condensed) | < 1 μm | Welding fume |
| Dust | Solid (mechanical) | 1 – 100 μm | Coal dust |
| Mist | Liquid droplet | 1 – 10 μm | Acid mist |
| Vapor | Liquid (evaporated) | Molecular | Solvent vapor |
| Gas | Gas | Molecular | Chlorine, CO |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metrics (must memorize):**
> - **TLV-TWA** = **5 mg/m³** (welding fume general)
> - **TLV-TWA** = **0.05 mg/m³** (lead, inorganic)
> - **TLV-STEL** = **10 mg/m³** (welding fume)
> - **PEL (OSHA)** = **0.05 mg/m³** (lead)
> - **IDLH (lead)** = **100 mg/m³**
> - **Conversion factor at 25 °C, 1 atm:** 1 ppm = (MW / 24.45) mg/m³
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Exposure Pathway Model

Industrial hygiene follows the **Source–Path–Receiver** model:

1. **Source** — Where the fume is generated (welding torch, melting pot, smelter).
2. **Path** — How it travels through air (dispersion, ventilation currents).
3. **Receiver** — The worker's breathing zone.

**The Why:** Control measures are tiered — the most effective (and KTU's preferred answer) intervene at the **source**, the next at the **path**, and the least reliable at the **receiver** (PPE).

## 2.2 Exposure Routes

- **Inhalation** (primary route — 90 % of fume uptake).
- **Skin/Eye contact** (especially soluble metal salts).
- **Ingestion** (mucociliary clearance → swallowing; hand-to-mouth).

## 2.3 Acute vs. Chronic Effects

- **Acute** — Rapid onset (metal fume fever, respiratory irritation, CNS depression from solvents).
- **Chronic** — Long-term (siderosis from iron, lead neuropathy, silicosis from contaminated fume, cancer from Cr(VI), berylliosis, cadmium emphysema).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` for absolute value in inline math to keep markdown tables intact.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|------------------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Haber's Law** (dose-response) | $C \times t = k$ | C = concentration, t = time, k = constant for fixed response |
| 2 | Ventilation air required (general) | $Q = \dfrac{K \cdot G}{C_{TLV} - C_{0}}$ | Q = m³/s, G = generation rate mg/s, K = safety factor |
| 3 | Capture velocity (canopy hood) | $V_{c} = \dfrac{Q}{10 \cdot A_{hood}}$ | V_c in m/s, A in m² |
| 4 | Slot hood entry velocity | $V = \dfrac{Q}{L \cdot W}$ | L = slot length, W = slot width |
| 5 | ppm → mg/m³ conversion (25 °C, 1 atm) | $\text{mg/m}^3 = \dfrac{\text{MW}}{24.45} \times \text{ppm}$ | 24.45 = molar volume L/mol |
| 6 | mg/m³ → ppm conversion | $\text{ppm} = \dfrac{24.45}{\text{MW}} \times \text{mg/m}^3$ | At 25 °C, 1 atm |
| 7 | **ACGIH Additive Mixture Formula** | $\dfrac{C_{1}}{TLV_{1}} + \dfrac{C_{2}}{TLV_{2}} + \dots \le 1$ | Applies to substances with **similar** toxic action |
| 8 | Piston model (lung deposition) | $V_{dep} = f \times C \times t \times V_{resp}$ | f = deposition fraction, V_resp = minute ventilation |
| 9 | Effective dose | $D = C \times IR \times t$ | IR = inhalation rate (~0.83 m³/h for adult worker) |
| 10 | TLV compliance check | $E = \dfrac{C_{1}T_{1} + C_{2}T_{2} + \dots}{8\,\text{h}}$ | E = 8-h TWA exposure |

## 2.5 The Hierarchy of Controls (Highly Testable)

In descending order of effectiveness:

1. **Elimination** — Stop the process.
2. **Substitution** — Use less toxic material (e.g., lead-free solder).
3. **Engineering Controls** — Local exhaust ventilation (LEV), enclosure, fume cupboards.
4. **Administrative Controls** — Job rotation, training, work-rest cycles.
5. **PPE** — Respirators (last resort).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Any 14-mark question on "control of hazardous fumes" MUST list the hierarchy, then expand on LEV (local exhaust ventilation) as the primary engineering control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Haber's Law (Dose-Response for Fixed Effect)

Haber's Law (1924) was originally proposed for chemical warfare agents and later extended to industrial hygiene. It assumes that **for a given intensity of biological response, the product of concentration and time of exposure is constant.**

### Starting Premise

For a fixed toxicological effect (e.g., 50 % mortality in test animals, or a defined irritation threshold), the relationship between concentration and exposure time can be written empirically as:

$$
C^{n} \times t = k
$$

where $n$ is a substance-specific exponent (often close to 1 for irritants, 2 – 3 for cumulative toxins).

### Step 1: Assume First-Order Response

For most industrial fumes, empirical data fit $n = 1$, so the equation simplifies to:

$$
C \times t = k
$$

### Step 2: Solving for Required Time at Known Concentration

Given TLV-TWA = $C_{TLV}$ for 8 hours, the constant $k$ becomes:

$$
k = C_{TLV} \times t_{ref} = C_{TLV} \times 8
$$

So for any other concentration $C_{x}$, the equivalent exposure time to produce the *same biological effect* is:

$$
t_{x} = \dfrac{C_{TLV} \times 8}{C_{x}}
$$

### Step 3: Numerical Example

A worker is exposed to lead fume at $C_{x} = 0.20\,\text{mg/m}^3$ (vs. TLV-TWA of $0.05\,\text{mg/m}^3$). Calculate the equivalent exposure time that delivers the same dose as an 8-hour TWA exposure.

**Logic:** We are equating biological dose, so use Haber's relation.

$$
t_{x} = \dfrac{C_{TLV} \times 8}{C_{x}} = \dfrac{0.05 \times 8}{0.20}
$$

$$
t_{x} = \dfrac{0.40}{0.20} = 2.0\,\text{hours}
$$

**Interpretation:** Working 2 hours at 0.20 mg/m³ delivers the same internal dose as working 8 hours at the TLV. The worker's shift must therefore be **restricted to ≤ 2 hours** if no engineering controls are applied.

### Step 4: Limitation of Haber's Law

The law **fails** for:
- **Cumulative toxins** (lead, mercury) where dose accumulates in bone/tissue.
- **Carcinogens** with no threshold.
- **Reactive gases** (ozone, NO₂) where the body adapts.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not blindly apply Haber's Law to lead or asbestos — these are cumulative toxins where the body's burden builds up over years, not hours. Examiners award partial credit for noting the limitation.

## 3.2 Derivation of Ventilation Air Quantity (Q)

### Setup

A process generates hazardous fume at a constant rate $G$ (mg/s). Outside (incoming) air contains concentration $C_{0}$ (mg/m³). The required ventilation rate $Q$ (m³/s) dilutes the contaminant to a level at or below the TLV.

### Mass Balance on the Workroom

$$
\text{Input rate} + \text{Generation rate} = \text{Output rate}
$$

$$
Q \cdot C_{0} + G = Q \cdot C_{TLV}
$$

### Rearranging for Q

$$
G = Q \cdot (C_{TLV} - C_{0})
$$

$$
Q = \dfrac{G}{C_{TLV} - C_{0}}
$$

### Applying a Safety Factor K

Engineering practice (ACGIH Industrial Ventilation Manual) introduces $K \ge 1$ to account for non-uniform generation, hood inefficiency, and worker location:

$$
Q = \dfrac{K \cdot G}{C_{TLV} - C_{0}}
$$

### Numerical Example

A welding operation generates iron oxide fume at $G = 100\,\text{mg/s}$. The TLV-TWA is $C_{TLV} = 5\,\text{mg/m}^3$ and incoming air is clean ($C_{0} = 0$). Use safety factor $K = 1.5$.

$$
Q = \dfrac{1.5 \times 100}{5 - 0} = \dfrac{150}{5} = 30\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Convert to CFM (1 m³/s = 2118.88 CFM):

$$
Q = 30 \times 2118.88 \approx 63{,}566\,\text{CFM}
$$

## 3.3 ACGIH Additive Mixture Calculation

When multiple fumes with **similar toxic action** are present (e.g., multiple solvents depressing the CNS), the combined exposure must be evaluated:

$$
\dfrac{C_{1}}{TLV_{1}} + \dfrac{C_{2}}{TLV_{2}} + \dots + \dfrac{C_{n}}{TLV_{n}} \le 1
$$

If the sum exceeds 1, the mixture exceeds the TLV even if no single substance does.

### Worked Example

A paint shop contains:
- Toluene: $C_{1} = 50\,\text{ppm}$, $TLV_{1} = 20\,\text{ppm}$
- Xylene: $C_{2} = 30\,\text{ppm}$, $TLV_{2} = 100\,\text{ppm}$

$$
S = \dfrac{50}{20} + \dfrac{30}{100} = 2.5 + 0.3 = 2.8
$$

Since $S = 2.8 > 1$, the mixture **exceeds the TLV** by a factor of 2.8. Engineering controls (LEV) are mandatory.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Industrial Fumes

| Fume | Source | TLV-TWA | Acute Effect | Chronic Effect |
|------|--------|---------|--------------|----------------|
| Iron oxide (welding) | Arc welding, cutting | 5 mg/m³ | Metal fume fever | Siderosis (benign) |
| Manganese | Steel welding | 0.1 mg/m³ | CNS effects | Parkinsonian syndrome |
| Lead | Solder, smelting | 0.05 mg/m³ | Colic, anemia | Neuropathy, nephropathy |
| Chromium (VI) | Stainless welding | 0.005 mg/m³ | Nasal irritation | Lung cancer (Group 1) |
| Cadmium | Smelting, Ni-Cd battery | 0.01 mg/m³ | Pneumonitis | Emphysema, cancer |
| Zinc oxide | Galvanizing, brazing | 2 mg/m³ | Metal fume fever | Usually resolves |
| Beryllium | Aerospace, nuclear | 0.00005 mg/m³ | Pneumonitis | Berylliosis, cancer |
| HF (from polymer) | Plastics burning | 0.5 ppm | Pulmonary edema | Skeletal fluorosis |
| Phosgene | Thermal decomposition of chlorinated solvents | 0.1 ppm | Delayed edema | Bronchitis |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fume Exposure Pathway (Source → Receiver)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Fume Source Welding Torch] --> airA[Airborne Dispersion]
    airA --> airB[Pathway Through Ventilation Current]
    airB --> airC[Worker Breathing Zone]
    airC --> airD[Inhalation and Lung Deposition]
    airD --> airE[Systemic Absorption Blood]
    airE --> outA[Acute Effects]
    airE --> outB[Chronic Health Outcomes]
```

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Controls for Fume Exposure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    lvl1[Elimination Stop the Process] --> lvl2[Substitution Less Hazardous Material]
    lvl2 --> lvl3[Engineering Controls Local Exhaust Ventilation Enclosure]
    lvl3 --> lvl4[Administrative Controls Job Rotation Training]
    lvl4 --> lvl5[PPE Respirators Last Resort]
    style lvl1 fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff
    style lvl2 fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff
    style lvl3 fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff
    style lvl4 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#ffffff
    style lvl5 fill:#d62728,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Color Key:** Blue tiers = most effective; Orange = supportive; Red = last-resort PPE.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Local Exhaust Ventilation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    procA[Hot Process Welding Melting] --> fanA[Fume Generation Hood Capture]
    fanA --> ductA[Ductwork with Transport Velocity]
    ductA --> fanB[Exhaust Fan]
    fanB --> filA[Air Cleaning Device Cyclone Bag Filter HEPA Scrubber]
    filA --> stackA[Stack Discharge to Atmosphere]
    filA -. filtered air .-> recA[Optional Recirculation After Monitoring]
    recA --> roomA[Return to Workroom]
    procA -. makeup air .-> roomA
    roomA -. makeup air .-> fanA
```

## 4.4 Worker Respiratory Protection Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1[What is the Contaminant] --> q2{IDLH Level}
    q2 -- Yes --> r1[SCBA or Supplied Air with Escape Bottle]
    q2 -- No --> q3{Concentration vs TLV}
    q3 -- Less than 10 times TLV --> r2[Air Purifying Half Mask APR]
    q3 -- 10 to 50 times TLV --> r3[Full Face APR with Specific Cartridge]
    q3 -- 50 to 100 times TLV --> r4[Powered Air Purifying Respirator PAPR]
    q3 -- More than 100 times TLV --> r5[Supplied Air Respirator SAR]
    r1 --> fit[Fittest Quantitative or Qualitative Fit Test]
    r2 --> fit
    r3 --> fit
    r4 --> fit
    r5 --> fit
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visual Note:** The American Conference of Governmental Industrial Hygienists (ACGIH) recommends that respirators be selected using the **Assigned Protection Factor (APF)** matched to the hazard ratio $C / TLV$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between a fume, a dust, and a vapor. Give one industrial example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **fume** is a solid aerosol formed by condensation of vaporized material, typically < 1 μm in size. Example: iron oxide fume from arc welding.

A **dust** is a solid aerosol generated by mechanical disintegration of a parent material, size 1 – 100 μm. Example: silica dust from grinding.

A **vapor** is the gaseous phase of a substance that is normally liquid at room temperature. Example: toluene vapor in a paint shop.

*(1 mark each for definition + correct example; 3 marks total.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**State Haber's Law. Why is it not valid for cumulative toxins like lead?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Haber's Law states that for a fixed biological response, the product of exposure concentration and exposure time is constant: $C \times t = k$.

*(1 mark for the statement.)*

It is **not valid for cumulative toxins** such as lead because these substances accumulate in body tissues (bone, kidney) over years, and the toxic effect depends on the **total body burden** rather than the instantaneous $C \times t$ product. Hence short, high exposures and long, low exposures cannot be equated by Haber's Law. Carcinogens and reactive gases also violate the law.

*(2 marks for the limitation.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]

#### Part (a) — 7 marks
**Explain the various types of hazardous fumes commonly encountered in industries. Discuss their sources, particle characteristics, and health effects.**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Metal Fumes (e.g., Fe, Mn, Cr, Zn, Pb, Cd, Be)** — Generated by welding, soldering, smelting, and metallurgical refining. Particle size < 1 μm, so they reach the **alveolar region** of the lungs. Health effects range from acute metal fume fever (Zn) to chronic neurological damage (Mn, Pb) and lung cancer (Cr-VI, Be). (3 marks)

2. **Polymer Fumes (HF, HCl, CO, isocyanates, dioxins)** — Formed by thermal decomposition of plastics, PVC, polyurethanes. HF and HCl are corrosive; isocyanates cause occupational asthma. (2 marks)

3. **Solvent Vapours (Benzene, Toluene, Xylene, TCE)** — From paints, adhesives, degreasing. They are vapours rather than solid fumes but are commonly grouped under hazardous airborne contaminants. CNS depression; benzene is a Group 1 carcinogen (leukemia). (1 mark)

4. **Welding Fumes (mixed metal oxides, NOₓ, ozone)** — Complex mixtures with synergistic toxicity. (1 mark)

---

#### Part (b) — 7 marks
**A welding booth generates iron oxide fume at 80 mg/s. The TLV-TWA for iron oxide is 5 mg/m³. Calculate the minimum exhaust ventilation flow rate required. State two engineering controls for fume control. Use safety factor K = 1.25 and assume clean incoming air.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $G = 80\,\text{mg/s}$
- $C_{TLV} = 5\,\text{mg/m}^3$
- $C_{0} = 0$ (clean air)
- $K = 1.25$

**Formula:**

$$
Q = \dfrac{K \cdot G}{C_{TLV} - C_{0}}
$$

**Substitution:**

$$
Q = \dfrac{1.25 \times 80}{5 - 0} = \dfrac{100}{5} = 20\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**In CFM:** $20 \times 2118.88 = 42{,}378\,\text{CFM}$

**Engineering controls:** (i) Local exhaust ventilation with a flanged hood placed within 1 duct diameter of the arc. (ii) Substitution of high-Mn electrodes with low-Mn alternatives. (Bonus 1 mark for mentioning enclosure or fume extractor with HEPA filtration.)

---

### Question B (Internal Choice) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]

#### Part (a) — 7 marks
**Describe the role of Threshold Limit Values (TLV) in industrial hygiene. Explain TLV-TWA, TLV-STEL, and TLV-C with suitable examples.**

**Model Solution:**

**TLV** = Threshold Limit Value, published annually by the ACGIH, representing airborne concentrations to which nearly all workers may be exposed day after day without adverse health effects. (1 mark)

- **TLV-TWA (Time-Weighted Average):** 8-hour time-weighted average exposure. Example: lead = 0.05 mg/m³; iron oxide = 5 mg/m³. (2 marks)
- **TLV-STEL (Short-Term Exposure Limit):** 15-minute TWA that should not be exceeded even if 8-h TWA is within limits. Example: welding fume STEL = 10 mg/m³. (2 marks)
- **TLV-C (Ceiling):** Instantaneous concentration that must never be exceeded. Example: hydrogen fluoride TLV-C = 2 ppm. (2 marks)

---

#### Part (b) — 7 marks
**A worker is exposed to a mixture of two solvents in an adhesive unit:**
- Solvent A: $C_A = 30\,\text{ppm}$, $TLV_A = 50\,\text{ppm}$
- Solvent B: $C_B = 40\,\text{ppm}$, $TLV_B = 100\,\text{ppm}$

**Both have similar CNS-depressant action. Using the ACGIH additive formula, determine whether the exposure is within the TLV. If the worker uses a half-mask APR with APF = 10, does the respirator provide adequate protection?**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Additive Mixture Index**

$$
S = \dfrac{C_A}{TLV_A} + \dfrac{C_B}{TLV_B} = \dfrac{30}{50} + \dfrac{40}{100} = 0.6 + 0.4 = 1.0
$$

Since $S = 1.0$, the exposure is **at the TLV limit** (just compliant). (3 marks)

**Step 2: Hazard Ratio for Respirator Selection**

Hazard ratio = $S \times$ (multiple of TLV). For a mixture at TLV, ratio = 1.0. Worker exposure concentration = 1.0 × TLV.

Required APF = exposure / TLV = 1.0.

**Half-mask APR (APF = 10)** provides protection up to 10 × TLV, which is far greater than required. (2 marks)

**Conclusion:** The respirator is **adequate** but administrative controls (improving ventilation) are recommended to bring $S < 0.5$ for better safety margin. (2 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. Forgetting to include the **safety factor K** in ventilation Q calculation (−2 marks).
> 2. Using Haber's Law for **lead or asbestos** without stating its limitation (−1 mark).
> 3. Confusing **TLV-TWA with TLV-C** (ceiling is never to be exceeded) (−1 mark).
> 4. Forgetting to convert **m³/s to CFM** when the question expects CFM (−1 mark).
> 5. Applying additive mixture formula to substances with **dissimilar** toxic action (invalid; use only for similar mode of action) (−1 mark).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Fumes are solid sub-micron particles** from condensation; distinct from dusts, mists, vapors, and gases.
- **Source–Path–Receiver** is the fundamental industrial-hygiene model.
- **TLV-TWA (8 h), TLV-STEL (15 min), TLV-C (instantaneous)** are the three ACGIH TLV types.
- **Haber's Law** $C \times t = k$ works for **non-cumulative irritants only**.
- **Ventilation Q** $= K \cdot G / (C_{TLV} - C_{0})$ is the workhorse formula.
- **Additive mixture formula** applies only to substances with **similar toxic action**.
- **PPE is the last tier** in the hierarchy of controls; engineering controls always preferred.
- **Conversion:** $1\,\text{ppm} = (MW / 24.45)\,\text{mg/m}^3$ at 25 °C, 1 atm.
- **Capture velocity** must match release type (e.g., 0.5 – 1.0 m/s for welding thermal plume).
- **Welding fume TLV** = 5 mg/m³ TWA / 10 mg/m³ STEL; lead = 0.05 mg/m³ TWA.
- **Carcinogenic fumes** (Cr-VI, beryllium, cadmium) have no safe threshold — ALARP principle applies.
- **Transport velocity** in ducts typically 15 – 20 m/s for fumes to prevent settling.
- **Filter selection** — P100 (HEPA equivalent) for metal fumes; combination cartridges for acid gases and organic vapors.
- **IDLH** value triggers **SCBA** or supplied-air respirator selection.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Source of Fumes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT834 - HAZARD CONTROL IN INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Source of Fumes # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Fumes In industrial hygiene and occupational safety engineering, **fumes** are defined as **fine, solid airborne particulates** generated by the **condensation of vaporized materials** (typically metals) into the air. They are formed when solid materials — such as molten metals, metallic alloys, or heated oxides — are heated to a temperature above their boiling point, vaporize, and subsequently undergo rapid oxidation and condensation in the cooler ambient atmosphere. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition Highlight** > Fumes are sub-microscopic solid particles (< 1 µm in aerodynamic diameter) formed by the **physical condensation of a vapor** (usually metallic) on contact with air. They are finer than dust and are typically produced by **thermal processes** such as welding, smelting, soldering, and metal cutting. According to standard occupational hygiene classifications, airborne contaminants in workplaces are broadly categorized into four groups: | Contaminant Type | Physical State | Origin Mechanism | Typical Particle Size | |---|---|---|---| | **Dust** | Solid (mechanical) | Crushing, grinding, abrasion, blasting | 1 µm – 100 µm | | **Fume** | Solid (condensed vapor) | Welding, smelting, sublimation | < 1 µm (sub-micron) | | **Mist** | Liquid (suspended droplets) | Spraying, condensation, atomization | 1 µm – 200 µm | | **Vapour** | Gaseous (from liquid/solid) | Evaporation, boiling, sublimation | Molecular (< 0.001 µm) | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kettle Steam Mirror" Intuition Think of holding a cold mirror above a boiling kettle of water: - Water vapor (a gas) rises invisibly from the kettle. - When it touches the cold mirror, it **condenses** back into tiny visible droplets — that cloud of moisture is a perfect analogue for what happens when metal vapor hits cool air. **Industrial fumes behave identically — except the "vapor" is metal (e.g., zinc, lead, chromium), and the "cold mirror" is the ambient workplace air.** The result is a cloud of extremely fine, often toxic, metallic oxide particles that remain suspended for hours. > [!NOTE] > **Memory Anchor for Students** > *"DUST is dug up. FUMES are condensed up. MIST is sprayed up. VAPOUR evaporates up."* ## 1.3 Distinguishing Fumes from Related Particulates A common student pitfall is confusing fumes with smoke or dust. The differentiation rests on **three fundamental criteria**: 1. **Generation Process** — Fumes require a **phase change from solid → vapor → solid**; dust requires only mechanical disintegration. 2. **Particle Morphology** — Fumes are typically **spherical, agglomerated, often oxidized**, while dust particles are irregularly shaped fragments. 3. **Settling Velocity** — Due to their sub-micron size, fumes have **negligible settling velocities** (Stokes regime), enabling long airborne residence times and deep-lung alveolar penetration. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The behavior of airborne fumes is governed by several measurable parameters: - **Aerodynamic Diameter ($d_a$):** typically **< 1 µm** for true fumes - **Stokes Settling Velocity ($V_s$):** $V_s = 0.001$ to $0.01$ m/s for fume particles - **Threshold Limit Values (TLV):** set by ACGIH; for instance, **TLV-TWA for zinc oxide fume = 5 mg/m³**, lead fume = **0.05 mg/m³** - **Standard Reference Temperature:** $25\,^\circ\text{C}$, $1$ atm - **Dynamic Viscosity of Air ($\mu$):** $1.81 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{Pa·s}$ at $25\,^\circ\text{C}$ --- # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Physical Mechanism of Fume Formation The generation of fumes follows a **three-stage thermodynamic pathway**, which must be thoroughly understood for any control engineering application: ### Stage 1 — Vaporization (Endothermic) A solid (or liquid) metal is heated beyond its boiling point. The Clausius–Clapeyron relation governs the vapor pressure equilibrium: $$P_v = P_0 \cdot \exp\left[\frac{L_v}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_0} - \frac{1}{T}\right)\right]$$ where $L_v$ is the latent heat of vaporization and $R$ is the universal gas constant. ### Stage 2 — Oxidation (Exothermic) The metallic vapor encounters atmospheric oxygen and undergoes an instantaneous oxidation reaction. For example: $$2\,\text{Zn (g)} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{ZnO (s)} + \text{Heat}$$ This is why **welding fumes are typically metal oxides**, not pure metals. ### Stage 3 — Nucleation & Condensation The newly formed oxide molecules rapidly condense into clusters (homogeneous nucleation) or condense on existing particles (heterogeneous nucleation), forming agglomerates of sub-micron diameter. ## 2.2 Industrial Sources of Fumes Fumes are produced in nearly every heavy industry. The principal sources grouped by process type are: ### A. Welding and Cutting Operations | Welding/Cutting Type | Fume Generated | Critical Toxic Component | |---|---|---| | Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) | Iron oxide, manganese oxide | **MnO₂** (neurotoxin) | | Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW/MIG) | Iron, copper, zinc oxides | ZnO → metal fume fever | | Flux-Cored Arc Welding (FCAW) | Fluoride, silica, manganese | Fluorides (irritant) | | Oxy-fuel Cutting | Iron oxide | Fe₂O₃ (siderosis) | | Plasma Arc Cutting | Chromium, nickel, hex chrome | **Cr(VI)** (carcinogen) | | Soldering | Lead, tin, rosin | Pb (neurotoxin) | ### B. Metallurgical Operations - **Smelting** — sulfide ore roasting releases SO₂ and metal oxide fumes - **Refining** — blast furnaces and converters generate Fe₂O₃, SiO₂, and lead fumes - **Foundry pouring** — metal-mold reactions at > 1500 °C ### C. Plastic and Polymer Processing - **PVC pyrolysis** — releases HCl fume + dioxin precursors - **Polyurethane foaming** — releases isocyanate fumes - **PTFE degradation** (Teflon) — produces extremely toxic polymer fume fever agents above 300 °C ### D. Combustion Processes - **Internal combustion engines** — lead, carbon, PAH - **Incinerators** — heavy metal fumes from waste streams - **Diesel engines** — soot + sulfate fumes (DPM) ### E. Surface Treatment and Finishing - **Electroplating** — chromic acid mist/fume (Cr VI) - **Hot-dip galvanizing** — zinc chloride/oxide fumes - **Annealing** — scale and oxide fumes ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Governing Equations > [!IMPORTANT] > All equations below are **mandatory** for KTU 2024 Module 3 problem-solving. $$ \begin{array}{|l|l|l|} \hline \textbf{Quantity} & \textbf{Formula} & \textbf{Unit / Boundary} \\ \hline \text{Stokes Settling Velocity} & V_s = \dfrac{\rho_p \, d^2 \, g}{18 \, \mu} & V_s \leq 0.1\ \text{m/s} \\ \hline \text{TLV-based Safety Factor} & SF = \dfrac{C_\text{measured}}{TLV} & SF \geq 1 \Rightarrow \text{Action required} \\ \hline \text{Exposure Dose} & E = C \times T & [\text{mg·h/m}^3] \\ \hline \text{Ventilation Q (Dilution)} & Q = \dfrac{G}{TLV \cdot K} & [\text{m}^3/\text{s}] \\ \hline \text{Henry's Law (Vapour)} & C_a = k_H \cdot P_v & [\text{Pa·m}^3/\text{mol}] \\ \hline \text{Clausius-Clapeyron} & \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_v}{P_0}\right) = \dfrac{L_v}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_0}-\dfrac{1}{T}\right) & P_v \text{ in Pa} \\ \hline \text{Aerosol Penetration} & P(d) = 1 - \exp[-d^2 \cdot k] & \text{Alveolar fraction} \\ \hline \text{Fume Density (Aitken)} & N = N_0 \exp(-B \cdot t) & B = \text{coagulation coef.} \\ \hline \end{array} $$ > [!NOTE] > In the formula $V_s = \rho_p d^2 g / (18\mu)$, the symbol $\vert$ for absolute value is intentionally avoided in the table syntax to preserve KTU 2024's strict markdown rendering rule. Where absolute magnitudes appear in inline math, use $\lvert x \rvert$ instead. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Understanding fume sources is the **first step of the hierarchy of controls**: 1. **Elimination** — substitute low-fume process (e.g., MIG → laser welding) 2. **Engineering Control** — local exhaust ventilation (LEV) with extraction hoods at the arc 3. **Administrative Control** — rotation of welders to limit cumulative Mn exposure 4. **PPE** — powered air-purifying respirators (PAPR) with HEPA + acid gas cartridges These controls are mandated in Indian Factories Act 1948 (Sections 13, 14) and the OSH Code 2020. --- # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivative 1 — Stokes Settling Velocity of a Fume Particle The **terminal settling velocity** determines how long a fume particle remains airborne, directly impacting its inhalation hazard. **Starting condition:** A spherical particle of density $\rho_p$ falling under gravity through air of viscosity $\mu$. **Step 1 — Identify forces acting on the particle:** $$ F_{\text{gravity}} = m \cdot g = \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \, \rho_p \cdot g $$ $$ F_{\text{buoyancy}} = \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \, \rho_{\text{air}} \cdot g $$ $$ F_{\text{drag}} = 3\pi \mu d \, V_s \quad \text{(Stokes' law, valid for Re < 0.1)} $$ **Step 2 — Apply force balance at terminal velocity** (acceleration = 0): $$ F_{\text{gravity}} - F_{\text{buoyancy}} - F_{\text{drag}} = 0 $$ **Step 3 — Substitute the expressions:** $$ \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \, \rho_p \, g - \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \, \rho_{\text{air}} \, g - 3\pi \mu d \, V_s = 0 $$ **Step 4 — Simplify** by factorising the common terms: $$ \frac{\pi d^3 g}{6} (\rho_p - \rho_{\text{air}}) = 3 \pi \mu d \, V_s $$ **Step 5 — Solve for $V_s$** by cancelling $\pi$ and one factor of $d$: $$ V_s = \frac{d^2 \, g \, (\rho_p - \rho_{\text{air}})}{18 \, \mu} $$ **Step 6 — Since $\rho_p \gg \rho_{\text{air}}$ for metal oxide fume** ($\rho_p \sim 5000\ \text{kg/m}^3$), drop $\rho_{\text{air}}$: $$ V_s = \frac{\rho_p \, d^2 \, g}{18 \, \mu} $$ **Numerical Example (for ZnO fume):** Given $d = 0.3\ \mu\text{m} = 3 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}$, $\rho_p = 5600\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{Pa·s}$, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$. $$ V_s = \frac{5600 \times (3 \times 10^{-7})^2 \times 9.81}{18 \times 1.81 \times 10^{-5}} $$ $$ V_s = \frac{5600 \times 9 \times 10^{-14} \times 9.81}{3.258 \times 10^{-4}} $$ $$ V_s = \frac{4.945 \times 10^{-10}}{3.258 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.518 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m/s} $$ **Interpretation:** A ZnO fume of $0.3\ \mu\text{m}$ takes roughly **4.8 hours** to settle from a $2.5\ \text{m}$ ceiling — making it an almost permanently airborne, deeply respirable hazard. ## 3.2 Derivative 2 — Required Dilution Ventilation Flow Rate The general dilution equation used for designing LEV on fume sources: **Starting principle:** Mass-balance at steady state — generated fume rate = ventilated fume rate. $$ G \left[\frac{\text{mg}}{\text{s}}\right] = Q \left[\frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{s}}\right] \times C_{\text{target}} \left[\frac{\text{mg}}{\text{m}^3}\right] $$ **Solving for Q:** $$ Q = \frac{G}{C_{\text{target}}} $$ **Safety-engineering form** with K (mixing factor, 3 ≤ K ≤ 10 for poor mixing) and TLV as the design concentration: $$ Q = \frac{G}{TLV \cdot K} $$ **Step-by-step example:** A welding station generates ZnO fume at $G = 50\ \text{mg/s}$. Required TLV-TWA $= 5\ \text{mg/m}^3$. Mixing factor $K = 5$ (poor mixing with hot plume). $$ Q = \frac{50}{5 \times 5} = 2\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 7200\ \text{m}^3/\text{h} $$ This is the **volumetric extraction rate** that the LEV hood must deliver. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Fume-Control Strategy | Process | Dominant Fume | Toxicity Tier | Recommended Primary Control | Typical Capture Velocity | |---|---|---|---|---| | SMAW (stick welding) | Fe₂O₃ + MnO₂ | Moderate | LEV + welding helmet PAPR | 0.5 – 1.0 m/s | | MIG/GMAW (galvanised) | ZnO | Moderate (fever) | LEV + push-pull ventilation | 0.5 – 1.5 m/s | | Plasma cutting stainless | Cr(VI) + Ni | **High (carcinogen)** | Enclosed cabin + HEPA exhaust | 1.0 – 2.5 m/s | | Soldering (Pb) | Pb + rosin | High | Local hood + charcoal filter | 0.5 – 1.0 m/s | | Foundry pouring | SiO₂ + metal oxide | High | Canopy hood + after-filter | 1.0 – 2.0 m/s | | PVC thermal cutting | HCl + dioxins | **Very High** | Total enclosure + scrubber | Enclosed system | | PTFE coating (decomp.) | PFOA, HF | **Extreme** | Forbidden above 300 °C | n/a (substitute) | ## 3.4 Fume Mass-Generation Correlation for Arc Welding Empirically, fume generation rate per unit arc time can be estimated from the **Lindell Formula** (commonly cited in industrial hygiene literature): $$ G = k \cdot I \cdot t_{\text{arc}} $$ where $I$ = welding current (Amperes), $t_{\text{arc}}$ = arc-on time (seconds), $k$ = empirical coefficient ($\sim 0.06$ – $0.35$ mg/A·s for SMAW). **Worked Example:** A welder uses SMAW at 200 A for 8 hours (with $t_{\text{arc}}$ factor 0.4). $$ G = 0.20 \times 200 \times (8 \times 3600 \times 0.4) $$ $$ G = 0.20 \times 200 \times 11520 = 460{,}800\ \text{mg} = 460.8\ \text{g fume / shift} $$ This emphasizes the **enormous mass of fine particles** produced — without LEV, TLV is exceeded within seconds. --- # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Fume Generation → Exposure → Control ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Source Material
Solid Metal or Alloy] --> B[Heat Source
Arc Furnace or Flame] B --> C[Stage 1 Vaporization
Liquid to Gas Phase] C --> D[Stage 2 Oxidation
Reaction with O2] D --> E[Stage 3 Condensation
Sub-micron Particulates] E --> F[Dispersion Cloud
Workplace Air] F --> G[Worker Exposure
Inhalation Pathway] G --> H[Health Effect
Acute or Chronic] H --> I[Engineering Control
LEV or Enclosure] I --> J[Filtered Discharge
Stack Emission] F --> K[Administrative Control
Rotation Training] G --> L[PPE
Respirator Hood] ``` ## 4.2 Schematic: Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) on a Welding Station ```mermaid flowchart LR W[Welding Arc
Fume Generation] --> H[Capture Hood
Slot Velocity 0.5 m/s] H --> D[Ductwork
Transport Velocity 15 m/s] D --> F[Pre-Filter
Spark Arrestor] F --> M[Main Filter
Cartridge or Baghouse] M --> C[Activated Carbon
Acid Gas Stage] C --> E[Exhaust Fan
2 m3/s Capacity] E --> S[Stack
10 m Above Roof] W --> R[Worker Zone
TLV Check] ``` ## 4.3 Classification Tree: Industrial Airborne Contaminants ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Industrial Airborne Contaminant] --> B[Solid Phase] A --> C[Liquid Phase] A --> D[Gas Phase] B --> B1[Dust
Mechanical Origin] B --> B2[Fume
Condensed Vapor] B --> B3[Smoke
Combustion Product] C --> C1[Mist
Suspended Droplets] C --> C2[Fog
Condensation Cloud] D --> D1[Vapor
From Liquid or Solid] D --> D2[Gas
Permanent State] ``` ## 4.4 Process–Fume Mapping Matrix (Hierarchical View) ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Industrial Process Family] --> P1[Metal Joining] P --> P2[Metal Forming] P --> P3[Surface Treatment] P --> P4[Polymer Processing] P1 --> F1[Welding Fumes
Fe Mn Cr Ni Zn] P1 --> F2[Soldering Fumes
Pb Sn Rosin] P2 --> F3[Foundry Fumes
SiO2 FeO MgO] P3 --> F4[Galvanizing Fumes
ZnO ZnCl2] P3 --> F5[Plating Fumes
CrO3 H2SO4 Mist] P4 --> F6[Plastic Fumes
HCl HCN Dioxins] F1 --> CTRL[LEV + PAPR] F2 --> CTRL F3 --> CTRL F4 --> CTRL F5 --> CTRL F6 --> CTRL ``` ## 4.5 Hazard-Critical Decision Logic for Inspector ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Fume Source Identified] --> Q1{Process Above 1000 C?} Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Material Contains
Cr Ni Pb Cd?} Q1 -->|No| Q3{Polymer or Coating
Involved?} Q2 -->|Yes| R1[Carcinogen Risk
TLV below 0.05 mg per m3] Q2 -->|No| R2[Standard Metal Fume
LEV sufficient] Q3 -->|Yes| R3[Acid or Toxic Gas
Scrubber Required] Q3 -->|No| R4[General Dilution
Ventilation Acceptable] ``` --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define "fumes" as applied to industrial hygiene. How are fumes distinguished from dust and mist in terms of (a) formation mechanism and (b) particle size?* **Model Answer:** Fumes are **fine, sub-micronic solid particulates** formed by the condensation of vaporized solids (typically metals) on contact with cooler air. They are produced by **thermal processes** such as welding, smelting, soldering, and metal cutting. (a) **Formation mechanism:** - *Fumes* — originate from a **solid → vapor → solid** phase change (condensation pathway). - *Dust* — originates from **mechanical disintegration** of solid matter (crushing, grinding, blasting). - *Mist* — originates from **liquid breakup** (atomization, spraying, condensation of vapour onto cool surfaces). (b) **Particle size:** - Fumes: < 1 µm (typically 0.001 – 1 µm) - Dust: 1 – 100 µm - Mist: 1 – 200 µm (liquid droplets) > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse smoke with fume. Smoke is a **combustion product containing carbon (soot) and unburnt volatiles**, while fume is a **condensed metal oxide**. Mark deduction if you interchange these in the answer. ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *List six major industrial sources of fumes and identify one characteristic toxic component from each.* **Model Answer:** | Sl. No. | Source | Characteristic Toxic Component | |---|---|---| | 1 | Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) | Manganese oxide (MnO₂) | | 2 | Stainless Steel Welding | Hexavalent Chromium Cr(VI) | | 3 | Soldering | Lead (Pb) | | 4 | Foundry Pouring | Crystalline silica (SiO₂) | | 5 | Hot-Dip Galvanizing | Zinc oxide (ZnO) | | 6 | PVC Thermal Cutting | Hydrogen chloride (HCl) | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **three-stage mechanism of fume formation** in welding. Discuss the role of oxidation in determining the chemical composition of welding fumes. **(b) [7 Marks]** A stainless-steel plasma cutting station generates Cr(VI) fumes at the rate of **$G = 5\ \text{mg/s}$**. The TLV-TWA for Cr(VI) is **$0.005\ \text{mg/m}^3$**. The mixing factor is $K = 6$. Calculate the **minimum exhaust ventilation flow rate** required to maintain the concentration at the worker's breathing zone at or below the TLV. Comment on the type of hood and capture velocity you would recommend. --- **Model Solution to Q.A(a):** The formation of welding fumes occurs in three thermodynamic stages: **Stage 1 — Vaporization:** The intense heat of the welding arc (5500 – 6500 °C) vaporizes the parent metal, the electrode, and any surface coating. For mild steel at arc temperature, the Clausius–Clapeyron relation gives: $$\ln\left(\frac{P_v}{P_0}\right) = \frac{L_v}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_0} - \frac{1}{T}\right)$$ **Stage 2 — Oxidation:** The metallic vapor reacts almost instantaneously with atmospheric oxygen. For example: $$2\,\text{Fe (g)} + \frac{3}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3\ (\text{s})$$ $$2\,\text{Cr (g)} + \frac{3}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3\ (\text{s})$$ **Stage 3 — Nucleation & Condensation:** The metal oxide molecules rapidly condense by homogeneous nucleation, forming **agglomerates of 0.01 – 1 µm diameter** that remain suspended in air. **Role of Oxidation:** The oxidation reaction determines that the fume is **not pure metal** but a **metal oxide**. The oxide's stoichiometry depends on local oxygen partial pressure, temperature, and alloy composition. This is why stainless-steel welding produces Cr(VI) (carcinogenic) while mild-steel welding produces Fe₂O₃ (relatively inert — siderosis). **Diagram:** *(Refer to Mermaid Block 4.1 in Section 4 of these notes.)* --- **Model Solution to Q.A(b):** Given: - $G = 5\ \text{mg/s}$ - $TLV_{Cr(VI)} = 0.005\ \text{mg/m}^3$ - $K = 6$ Using the dilution equation derived in Section 3.2: $$ Q = \frac{G}{TLV \cdot K} $$ $$ Q = \frac{5}{0.005 \times 6} = \frac{5}{0.03} = 166.67\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} $$ $$ Q = 166.67 \times 3600 = 6 \times 10^5\ \text{m}^3/\text{h} $$ **Conclusion:** A massive **$6 \times 10^5\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}$** extraction flow is needed. Because this is impractical with simple LEV, the **recommended solution is a fully enclosed cutting cabin** with negative pressure, fitted with a high-efficiency HEPA + chemical adsorption train. The capture velocity at any opening should be maintained at **1.0 – 2.5 m/s** to prevent Cr(VI) escape into the worker's breathing zone. --- ### Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **fumes, dust, mist, and vapour** as airborne industrial contaminants in a tabular form covering (i) physical state, (ii) typical size range, (iii) typical source process, and (iv) one health effect each. **(b) [7 Marks]** A galvanizing plant produces ZnO fumes. The fume particles have an average diameter of **$0.5\ \mu\text{m}$** and density of **$5600\ \text{kg/m}^3$**. Assuming **Stokes' regime**, calculate the **terminal settling velocity** in still air at $25\,^\circ\text{C}$ ($\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{Pa·s}$, $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.18\ \text{kg/m}^3$). What does this imply about the airborne residence time and inhalation hazard? --- **Model Solution to Q.B(a):** | Contaminant | Physical State | Typical Size | Source Process | Health Effect Example | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Fume** | Solid (condensed vapour) | < 1 µm | Welding, smelting | Metal fume fever (ZnO) | | **Dust** | Solid (mechanical) | 1 – 100 µm | Crushing, grinding | Silicosis (SiO₂ dust) | | **Mist** | Liquid (droplets) | 1 – 200 µm | Spraying, atomization | Respiratory irritation | | **Vapour** | Gaseous | Molecular | Solvent evaporation | CNS depression (toluene) | **Key distinguishing features:** - Only **fume** requires a solid-vapour-solid phase transition. - Only **mist** is a liquid in dispersed phase. - Only **vapour** is gaseous at room temperature. --- **Model Solution to Q.B(b):** Given: $d = 0.5\ \mu\text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}$, $\rho_p = 5600\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{Pa·s}$, $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.18\ \text{kg/m}^3$. Step 1 — Verify Stokes' regime (Reynolds number): $$ Re = \frac{\rho_{\text{air}} \, d \, V_s}{\mu} $$ First approximation using $V_s$ from the simplified equation. Step 2 — Apply Stokes' equation (full form): $$ V_s = \frac{d^2 \, g \, (\rho_p - \rho_{\text{air}})}{18 \, \mu} $$ Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values: $$ V_s = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-7})^2 \times 9.81 \times (5600 - 1.18)}{18 \times 1.81 \times 10^{-5}} $$ Step 4 — Compute numerator and denominator separately: **Numerator:** $$ (5 \times 10^{-7})^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-13} $$ $$ 2.5 \times 10^{-13} \times 9.81 = 2.4525 \times 10^{-12} $$ $$ 2.4525 \times 10^{-12} \times 5598.82 \approx 1.373 \times 10^{-8} $$ **Denominator:** $$ 18 \times 1.81 \times 10^{-5} = 3.258 \times 10^{-4} $$ **Step 5 — Final calculation:** $$ V_s = \frac{1.373 \times 10^{-8}}{3.258 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.21 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m/s} $$ Step 6 — Verify Stokes regime (Re < 0.1): $$ Re = \frac{1.18 \times 5 \times 10^{-7} \times 4.21 \times 10^{-5}}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 1.37 \times 10^{-6} \ll 0.1 $$ **Stokes regime confirmed.** Step 7 — Compute residence time for a $3\ \text{m}$ fall from ceiling: $$ t = \frac{h}{V_s} = \frac{3}{4.21 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 71{,}260\ \text{s} \approx 19.8\ \text{hours} $$ **Interpretation:** - A ZnO fume particle of $0.5\ \mu\text{m}$ takes **nearly 20 hours** to settle through a typical factory height. - It is therefore **permanently airborne** during an 8-hour shift. - Because $d < 4\ \mu\text{m}$, it penetrates to the **alveolar region**, causing **metal fume fever** and long-term pulmonary effects. - Engineering control (LEV) is mandatory — natural ventilation is **completely inadequate**. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Critical Loss Zone:** > 1. Students often **forget to subtract $\rho_{\text{air}}$** from $\rho_p$ — this yields a ~0.02 % error, which is acceptable, but **failing to convert $0.5\ \mu\text{m}$ into SI units ($5 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}$)** is a **2-mark penalty** in the KTU script. > 2. Not verifying the **Stokes regime validity (Re < 0.1)** loses 1 mark. > 3. Not providing the **engineering interpretation** (residence time + health implication) loses 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition Anchor:** Fumes are *condensed* solid particulates (< 1 µm) formed from vaporized solids — **not** mechanical debris. - **Three-Stage Pathway:** *Vaporize → Oxidize → Condense*. Always recall this sequence for KTU 4-mark descriptive questions. - **Top Exam-Relevant Sources:** Welding (Mn, Cr, Zn), Soldering (Pb), Foundry (SiO₂), Plasma cutting (Cr-VI), PVC processing (HCl). - **Carcinogen Watch-list:** **Cr(VI), Ni, Cd, Be** — these carry TLVs an order of magnitude lower than general fumes. - **Quantitative Toolkit:** Stokes settling ($V_s \propto d^2$), Dilution ventilation ($Q = G/(TLV \cdot K)$), Lindell fume generation ($G = k I t$). - **Particle Size → Penetration Rule:** $d < 4\ \mu\text{m}$ ⇒ alveolar; $d < 10\ \mu\text{m}$ ⇒ tracheobronchial; $d > 10\ \mu\text{m}$ ⇒ extrathoracic. - **Control Hierarchy:** **Substitute → LEV → Administrative → PPE**. KTU prefers LEV as primary, because PPE is the last line of defense. - **Statutory Linkages in India:** Factories Act 1948 (Sec 13, 14, 36); OSH Code 2020; ACGIH TLV booklet; IS 9588 (industrial ventilation). - **Always quote units in SI** in the KTU valuation script; mixed CGS units cause automatic half-mark deductions. - **Numerical Rule of Thumb:** For sub-micron fume ($d \sim 0.5\ \mu\text{m}$, $\rho_p \sim 5000\ \text{kg/m}^3$), expect $V_s \sim 10^{-5}\ \text{m/s}$ — committing this to memory helps short-answer estimation questions. ---

topics.title: Material Handling: Classification, Safety Consideration, Manual and Mechanical Handling, Handling Assessments and Techniques, Lifting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT834 - HAZARD CONTROL IN INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Material Handling: Classification, Safety Consideration, Manual and Mechanical Handling, Handling Assessments and Techniques, Lifting # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Material Handling — Formal Definition **Material Handling (MH)** is the systematic movement, protection, storage, and control of materials and products throughout the manufacturing, warehousing, distribution, consumption, and disposal stages of a workflow using scientific methods and engineered equipment. As per the **American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME)** and **Indian Factories Act, 1948 (Section 21–27)**, material handling encompasses the *art and science* of moving, packaging, and storing materials in any form. The **Crown (1981)** definition — adopted widely in KTU 2024 Scheme coursework — describes MH as: > *"The function of moving the right material to the right place, at the right time, in the right amount, in the right condition, and at the right cost."* ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Grocery Trip** > > Imagine you have to bring 30 kg of groceries from the car to the second-floor kitchen. You make **multiple short trips** instead of one heavy load (manual handling principle — distribute load). You use a **trolley** for heavy cartons and a **staircase ramp** instead of lifting straight up (mechanical handling aid). You **bend your knees**, not your back (posture / lifting technique). You avoid **twisting** while carrying (asymmetric lift avoidance). Material handling in industries follows exactly these same logical decisions — but scaled to tons of steel, chemicals, and machinery. ## 1.3 Why Material Handling Matters in Industry | Statistical Reality | Reference | |---|---| | **25% of all workplace injuries** are related to manual material handling | OSHA, USA | | **80,000+ lower-back injuries** annually due to improper lifting | NIOSH | | **30–40% of manufacturing cost** is attributed to material movement | IMH (Institute of Material Handling) | | **1 in 4 industrial accidents** involves material handling equipment | Indian Factory Inspectorate | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight** > Material Handling is a **CO4–CO5 mapped** topic under Hazard Control in Industries. Questions are typically asked for **3 marks (Part A definition)** and **14 marks (Part B classification + assessment problem)**. The **NIOSH Lifting Equation** is the single most repeated numerical problem in the module. ## 1.4 The 4M Classification Philosophy (Foundation Concept) All material handling activities can be categorized into four universal modes (the **"4M Principle"**): 1. **Move** — Changing location of material (truck, conveyor) 2. **Maintain** — Keeping material in place (racks, bins) 3. **Manufacture** — Processing material during handling (assembly line) 4. **Measure** — Quantifying material movement (KPI tracking) > [!TIP] > Every piece of material handling equipment — from a hand trolley to a 50-ton gantry crane — exists to serve one or more of these 4M functions. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Comprehensive Classification of Material Handling ### 2.1.1 Classification Based on **Type of Equipment** | Category | Description | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Manual Handling** | Powered entirely by human effort | Lifting, carrying, pushing, pulling | | **Mechanical Handling** | Uses human-operated machines | Hand trucks, pallet jacks, winches | | **Powered / Automated Handling** | Uses external power source | Conveyors, forklifts, cranes, AGVs | ### 2.1.2 Classification Based on **Function** | Type | Function | Example Equipment | |---|---|---| | **Transporting** | Move over distances | Trucks, conveyors, railways | | **Positioning** | Orient for next operation | Turntables, manipulators | | **Lifting** | Vertical movement | Cranes, hoists, elevators | | **Stacking/Storage** | Hold in reserve | Racks, pallets, bins | | **Unit Load Formation** | Group loose items | Pallets, skids, containers | ### 2.1.3 Classification Based on **Material Form** | Material State | Handling Method | |---|---| | Solids (unit) | Forklifts, conveyors, cranes | | Solids (bulk) | Belt conveyors, screw conveyors, bucket elevators | | Liquids | Pumps, pipelines, IBC tanks | | Gases | Compressors, pressurized cylinders | | Hazardous | Specialized containment + PPE | ## 2.2 Safety Considerations — The 7 Golden Rules > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Seven Pillars of Material Handling Safety (KTU Favourite)** > > 1. **Plan Before Lift** — Calculate load, route, and capability. > 2. **Inspect Equipment** — Pre-use checks on slings, brakes, hooks. > 3. **Clear Path** — Remove obstacles; ensure adequate lighting. > 4. **Maintain Posture** — Back straight, knees bent, feet shoulder-width. > 5. **Use Mechanical Aids** — Never lift > 25 kg manually (Indian Std / EU Directive). > 6. **Communicate** — Standard hand signals for crane operations. > 7. **PPE Compliance** — Helmets, steel-toe boots, gloves, high-vis vests. ## 2.3 Manual Material Handling (MMH) — Deep Dive ### 2.3.1 Definition (NIOSH, 1981) Manual material handling refers to tasks where one or more workers **lift, lower, push, pull, carry, hold, or restrain** an object without the assistance of mechanical equipment. ### 2.3.2 Causes of Manual Handling Injuries - **Overexertion** (> 25 kg lift, awkward posture) - **Repetitive motion** (frequency > 6 lifts/min) - **Static load holding** (muscle fatigue) - **Awkward postures** (twisting, bending, reaching) - **Vibration exposure** (hand-arm vibration syndrome) ### 2.3.3 Risk Factors (The "S-P-A-C-E" Mnemonic) | Letter | Factor | Description | |---|---|---| | **S** | Size of Load | Heavier = higher risk | | **P** | Posture | Awkward positions multiply spinal load | | **A** | Asymmetry | Twisting during lift = spinal disc shear | | **C** | Coupling | Poor grip = force multiplication | | **E** | Environment | Slippery floors, poor light, heat stress | ## 2.4 Mechanical Handling Equipment — Master Chart | Equipment | Capacity | Use Case | Hazard | |---|---|---|---| | **Forklift Truck** | 1–25 tons | Palletized loads | Tip-over, blind-spot crushing | | **Overhead Crane** | 1–500 tons | Heavy steel, machinery | Dropped load, swing impact | | **Gantry Crane** | 5–100 tons | Outdoor yards | Rail derailment | | **Jib Crane** | 0.5–10 tons | Workstation lifting | Boom collapse | | **Conveyor (Belt)** | Continuous | Bulk material | Entrapment, nip points | | **Roller Conveyor** | Continuous | Cartons | Pinch points | | **Chain Hoist** | 0.5–50 tons | Vertical lift | Chain failure | | **Pallet Jack** | 2–3 tons | Floor transport | Foot crush | ## 2.5 The NIOSH Lifting Equation — **THE** KTU Formula > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Revised NIOSH Lifting Equation (RNLE, 1991)** is the universally accepted biomechanical method to assess manual lifting tasks. **Master this — it appears in 90% of KTU numerical questions.** ### 2.5.1 Recommended Weight Limit (RWL) Formula $$ \text{RWL} = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM $$ Where **LC (Load Constant)** = **23 kg (51 lbs)** is the maximum recommended load under ideal conditions. ### 2.5.2 The Six Multipliers — Detailed Definitions | Multiplier | Symbol | Formula | Range | |---|---|---|---| | Horizontal Multiplier | $HM$ | $HM = \dfrac{25}{H}$ | $H$ = horizontal distance (cm) from spine mid-point to hands | | Vertical Multiplier | $VM$ | $VM = 1 - 0.003 \times \vert V - 75 \vert$ | $V$ = vertical height (cm) of hands from floor | | Distance Multiplier | $DM$ | $DM = 0.82 + \dfrac{1.8}{D}$ | $D$ = vertical travel distance (cm) of the load | | Asymmetry Multiplier | $AM$ | $AM = 1 - 0.0032 \times A$ | $A$ = asymmetry angle (degrees), 0° ≤ A ≤ 135° | | Frequency Multiplier | $FM$ | (Read from NIOSH table) | Depends on lifts/min and duration | | Coupling Multiplier | $CM$ | (Read from NIOSH table) | Depends on grip quality (Good/ Fair/ Poor) | ### 2.5.3 Lifting Index (LI) $$ LI = \frac{\text{Actual Load Weight (L)}}{\text{Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)}} $$ **Interpretation:** - **LI ≤ 1.0** → Safe for most workers - **1.0 < LI ≤ 3.0** → Increased risk; task redesign recommended - **LI > 3.0** → Severe risk; task must be redesigned ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Recommended Weight Limit | $RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$ | kg | | 2 | Load Constant | $LC = 23$ (revised NIOSH) | kg | | 3 | Horizontal Multiplier | $HM = \dfrac{25}{H}$ | dimensionless | | 4 | Vertical Multiplier | $VM = 1 - 0.003 \vert V - 75 \vert$ | dimensionless | | 5 | Distance Multiplier | $DM = 0.82 + \dfrac{1.8}{D}$ | dimensionless | | 6 | Asymmetry Multiplier | $AM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot A$ | dimensionless | | 7 | Lifting Index | $LI = \dfrac{L}{RWL}$ | dimensionless | | 8 | Spinal Compression Limit (action) | $\leq 3400$ N | Newtons | | 9 | Indian / EU Manual Lift Limit | $\leq 25$ kg (adult male, ideal conditions) | kg | | 10 | Maximum Static Push Force | $\leq 230$ N (women), $\leq 300$ N (men) | Newtons | ## 2.7 Frequency Multiplier (FM) Table — Compact Reference | Lifts / min | ≤ 1 hr | ≤ 2 hr | ≤ 8 hr | |---|---|---|---| | **0.2** | 1.00 | 0.95 | 0.85 | | **1** | 0.94 | 0.84 | 0.75 | | **3** | 0.84 | 0.72 | 0.55 | | **6** | 0.72 | 0.60 | 0.40 | | **12** | 0.45 | 0.30 | 0.20 | ## 2.8 Coupling Multiplier (CM) Values | Coupling Quality | V < 75 cm | V ≥ 75 cm | |---|---|---| | **Good** (handgrip, handles) | 1.00 | 1.00 | | **Fair** (loose object, partial grip) | 0.95 | 1.00 | | **Poor** (slippery, sharp edges, no grip) | 0.90 | 0.90 | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — NIOSH Lifting Equation (FULL SOLUTION) > [!NOTE] > **Problem (KTU-Standard Pattern)** > > A worker lifts a carton weighing **15 kg** from a shelf. Given data: > - Horizontal distance from spine to hands: **H = 30 cm** > - Vertical position of hands at start: **V = 50 cm** > - Vertical travel distance: **D = 60 cm** > - Asymmetry angle: **A = 30°** > - Frequency: **1 lift/min for ≤ 1 hour** → FM = 0.94 > - Coupling: **Fair** with V < 75 cm → CM = 0.95 > > **Calculate:** (a) Recommended Weight Limit (RWL) > (b) Lifting Index (LI) > (c) Comment on safety. ### Solution **Step 1 — Horizontal Multiplier (HM)** $$ HM = \frac{25}{H} = \frac{25}{30} = 0.833 $$ **Why?** The horizontal multiplier penalizes lifts where the load is held far from the body. As **H** increases, the load moment arm on the lumbar spine increases linearly, demanding greater muscular effort. **Step 2 — Vertical Multiplier (VM)** $$ VM = 1 - 0.003 \times \vert V - 75 \vert $$ Substituting **V = 50 cm**: $$ VM = 1 - 0.003 \times \vert 50 - 75 \vert $$ $$ VM = 1 - 0.003 \times 25 = 1 - 0.075 = 0.925 $$ **Why?** The optimal vertical lift height is 75 cm (waist level). Both too-low and too-high lifts reduce the multiplier. Here, the lift is below waist, so VM drops slightly. **Step 3 — Distance Multiplier (DM)** $$ DM = 0.82 + \frac{1.8}{D} = 0.82 + \frac{1.8}{60} $$ $$ DM = 0.82 + 0.030 = 0.850 $$ **Why?** Shorter travel distances are safer. The DM decreases as travel distance increases, because longer lifts cause greater cumulative fatigue. **Step 4 — Asymmetry Multiplier (AM)** $$ AM = 1 - 0.0032 \times A = 1 - 0.0032 \times 30 $$ $$ AM = 1 - 0.096 = 0.904 $$ **Why?** Twisting (asymmetric lifting) generates shear stress on intervertebral discs. Each degree of asymmetry linearly reduces the safe lifting capacity. **Step 5 — Frequency Multiplier (FM) and Coupling Multiplier (CM)** Given directly: $$ FM = 0.94, \quad CM = 0.95 $$ **Step 6 — Compute RWL** $$ RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM $$ $$ RWL = 23 \times 0.833 \times 0.925 \times 0.850 \times 0.904 \times 0.94 \times 0.95 $$ Computing step-by-step: $$ 23 \times 0.833 = 19.159 $$ $$ 19.159 \times 0.925 = 17.722 $$ $$ 17.722 \times 0.850 = 15.064 $$ $$ 15.064 \times 0.904 = 13.618 $$ $$ 13.618 \times 0.94 = 12.801 $$ $$ 12.801 \times 0.95 = 12.161 \text{ kg} $$ $$ \boxed{RWL \approx 12.16 \text{ kg}} $$ **Step 7 — Compute Lifting Index (LI)** $$ LI = \frac{L}{RWL} = \frac{15}{12.16} = 1.23 $$ $$ \boxed{LI = 1.23} $$ **Step 8 — Safety Interpretation** Since **1.0 < LI = 1.23 ≤ 3.0**, the task poses **increased risk** to some workers. KTU-mandated recommendation: **Redesign the task** (use a mechanical aid, reduce H, reduce frequency, or improve coupling). --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Reverse Problem (Finding Maximum Permissible Load) > A worker performs a symmetric lift (A = 0°) at H = 25 cm, V = 75 cm, D = 25 cm, with FM = 0.84, CM = 1.00. What is the **maximum safe weight** that 75% of female workers can lift? ### Solution **Step 1 — Compute each multiplier** $$ HM = \frac{25}{25} = 1.000 $$ $$ VM = 1 - 0.003 \times \vert 75 - 75 \vert = 1.000 $$ $$ DM = 0.82 + \frac{1.8}{25} = 0.82 + 0.072 = 0.892 $$ $$ AM = 1 - 0.0032 \times 0 = 1.000 $$ $$ FM = 0.84, \quad CM = 1.00 $$ **Step 2 — RWL calculation** $$ RWL = 23 \times 1.000 \times 1.000 \times 0.892 \times 1.000 \times 0.84 \times 1.00 $$ $$ RWL = 23 \times 0.892 \times 0.84 $$ $$ RWL = 23 \times 0.7493 = 17.234 \text{ kg} $$ $$ \boxed{RWL \approx 17.23 \text{ kg}} $$ This is the **Recommended Weight Limit** — meaning approximately **75% of the working population** can lift this safely. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Manual vs Mechanical Handling | Factor | Manual Preferred When | Mechanical Preferred When | |---|---|---| | Load Weight | < 15 kg, intermittent | > 25 kg, repetitive | | Distance | < 2 m | > 5 m or vertical > 1 m | | Frequency | < 1 lift/min | > 6 lifts/min | | Path | Cluttered, short | Open, repetitive routes | | Worker Skill | Trained, fit, healthy | Operator can use machine | | Cost (Initial) | Low setup cost | High but long-term ROI | | Flexibility | Variable tasks | Fixed repetitive tasks | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner Trick Question:** > *"Should mechanical handling always replace manual?"* > **Answer:** No. The **unit-load principle** by **Crown (1981)** emphasizes choosing the right method for the **right situation**. Over-mechanization adds cost and complexity; under-mechanization increases injury risk. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Material Handling Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef inputNode fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef processNode fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outputNode fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decisionNode fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 A[Material Input
